items.sourceNote: Inspired by 換日線 article on Japanese complaint-handling master — rewritten as an ironic 'smile is the cheapest armor' observation from the service worker's POV, original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
面試官：「請問你最大的優點是？」
我：「我會在被罵的時候微笑。」
面試官：「太好了，明天就來上班。」
我：「⋯⋯這真的算優點嗎？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 服務業徵才條件第一名是「抗壓性」，翻譯成白話就是「會在崩潰邊緣繼續笑」。傳給那位昨天面試完才發現自己被錄取真的不是好事的朋友。
variations: 「抗壓性高」是服務業最危險的讚美，等於說你還可以再被多踩一腳。
微笑是服務業最便宜的盔甲，所以公司才會發那麼多。

en.content:
Interviewer: "What's your greatest strength?"
Me: "I smile while being yelled at."
Interviewer: "Perfect. Start tomorrow."
Me: "...Wait, is that actually a strength?"
en.editorNote: The #1 service-industry hiring criterion is "stress tolerance," which translates to "can keep smiling on the edge of a breakdown." Send this to the friend who finished the interview and only later realized getting hired wasn't actually good news.
variations: "High stress tolerance" is the most dangerous compliment in service work — it means there's still room to step on you harder.
A smile is the cheapest armor in service work, which is why the company hands them out for free.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 經理人 2026 新竹奧客事件評論「寧可得罪奧客，也不要氣走員工」 management角度； rewritten from employee POV as a 「公司現在開始護員工，是因為徵不到下一個」 cynical reframe — original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
以前主管說：「客人最大，先道歉再說。」
現在主管說：「不要忍，公司挺你。」
中間發生了什麼事？
中間發生的是——
他們發現徵不到下一個我了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近發現公司突然開始「保護員工」的同事。不是公司變好了，是缺工讓你變貴了。傳給對「公司新政策」抱持希望的菜鳥，溫柔提醒一下。
variations: 公司開始挺員工，不是因為良心發現，是因為履歷表變薄了。
「不要忍」的前提，是公司知道再徵一個比安撫客人還貴。

en.content:
Old manager line: "Customer is king. Apologize first, sort it out later."
New manager line: "Don't take it. We've got your back."
What happened in between?
In between, they figured out
they couldn't hire the next me.
en.editorNote: Send this to anyone who suddenly noticed the company is "protecting employees" now. The company didn't grow a conscience — the labor shortage made you expensive. A gentle reality check for newer coworkers who still believe in "new policies."
variations: Companies start protecting employees not because they found a conscience, but because the résumé stack got thin.
"Don't take it" only kicks in once the company realizes hiring your replacement costs more than soothing a complaint.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by BuzzFeed 2026-05-19 retail-worker wild-stories roundup; rewritten as a 「客人把店當自家客廳」 boundary-blurring joke — original phrasing avoiding direct anecdote copy

zh-tw.content:
客人坐在試衣間外面的沙發上，
脫掉鞋、滑手機、
叫他老公去拿衣服、
吃自己帶的麵包配可樂。
我走過去禮貌地問：「請問需要協助嗎？」
他抬頭看我：「妳很吵欸。」
抱歉，這是賣場，不是您家的二樓。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「客人把店當客廳」攻擊過的零售同事。我們提供更衣間、試穿椅、空調，但沒有附贈一個「家」的感覺給您。
variations: 客人把賣場當自己家，唯一沒有的是房貸壓力。
您在這裡可以試穿、可以休息，但不能脫鞋、不能野餐、不能罵店員。

en.content:
Customer sitting on the fitting-room couch:
shoes off, scrolling her phone,
ordering her husband to fetch outfits,
eating a sandwich she brought from home, washing it down with a Coke.
I walked over: "Can I help you with anything?"
She looked up: "You're being loud."
Ma'am, this is a department store. Not the second floor of your house.
en.editorNote: Send to retail coworkers recently ambushed by the "customer who treats the store like a living room" species. We provide fitting rooms, benches, air conditioning — not a complimentary feeling of home.
variations: She treats the store like her own house — minus the mortgage stress.
Here you may try things on, sit, cool off. You may not take off your shoes, eat a picnic, or yell at the staff.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Pleated Jeans 2026-05-11 sushi-restaurant Kayla roundup; rewritten as 「客人覺得我們是 24 小時待命的 AI」 modern-tech-expectation joke — original framing

zh-tw.content:
客人晚上十一點傳訊：「在嗎？」
隔天早上八點半，他第一通電話打進來：「為什麼昨天沒回我？」
先生，我們是服務業，不是 ChatGPT，
我們會下班、會睡覺、會夢到您罵我們。
您不能 24 小時連線到我，
您家的 Wi-Fi 都不一定 24 小時穩定了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「為什麼沒馬上回」攻擊過的客服與小編同事。AI 都會當機重啟了，何況是領時薪的人。
variations: 客人以為員工是雲端服務，隨時 online、永遠不離線、出錯還不能退費。
您家的 Wi-Fi 都會斷線，憑什麼我們不能下班。

en.content:
Customer texts at 11pm: "You there?"
8:30am, his first call comes in: "Why didn't you reply yesterday?"
Sir, we're a service business. Not ChatGPT.
We clock out, we sleep, we dream about you yelling at us.
You cannot stay connected to me 24/7.
Even your home Wi-Fi isn't connected 24/7.
en.editorNote: Send to customer-support coworkers and social-media managers who got hit with "why didn't you reply instantly?" If AI gets to crash and reboot, an hourly worker definitely does.
variations: Customers think employees are cloud services: always online, never down, no refund on errors.
Your Wi-Fi drops out. Somehow I'm not allowed to clock out.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 方格子 「客人就是欺善怕惡」 essay; rewritten as 「客人對 AI 客服很有耐心，對真人客服沒有」 modern技術倒置 framing — original anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人跟 AI 客服機器人講話，會說「請」、會說「謝謝」、
還會打字打很慢怕它聽不懂。
轉接到真人客服，第一句話：「終於是人了，你給我聽好。」
原來機器人比人類受尊重，
因為機器人不會哭，
客人罵爽就好。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給做客服的同行——尤其是那些被「終於是人了」開頭的電話折磨過的。AI 時代最諷刺的事情：機器人擁有的禮貌，比真人客服還多。
variations: 客人對 AI 講話比對真人客氣，因為他知道 AI 沒有眼淚可以收。
機器人不會委屈，所以客人捨不得對它兇——這是 2026 年最荒謬的禮貌邏輯。

en.content:
When customers talk to the AI chatbot, they say "please," they say "thanks,"
they type slowly in case the bot can't keep up.
Transferred to a human agent, first sentence: "Finally, a real person. Now you listen to me."
Turns out robots get more respect than people,
because robots don't cry,
so the customer can yell guilt-free.
en.editorNote: Send to call-center coworkers — especially anyone who got worn down by a call that opened with "finally, a real person." The most ironic thing about the AI era: the chatbot gets more politeness than the human.
variations: Customers are politer to AI than to humans, because they know AI has no tears to collect.
Robots can't feel hurt, so customers feel bad yelling at them. That's the dumbest politeness logic of 2026.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 心情板 「奧客經典語錄」 roundup; rewritten as 「您知道我是誰嗎？」 self-introduction punchline — original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
奧客：「妳知道我是誰嗎？」
我笑著回：「不好意思，這位先生，
我這邊有客戶資料系統可以查，
您要不要報一下您的會員編號？」
奧客愣了三秒：「⋯⋯算了。」
原來「我是誰」這個問題，
他自己也答不出來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「您知道我是誰嗎？」綁架的櫃台同事。會員系統是這個時代最有效的奧客照妖鏡——真正的 VIP 不需要自報名號，他的編號會幫他講話。
variations: 問「你知道我是誰嗎？」的客人，通常就是那個沒有人需要記住的人。
真正的 VIP 不會問你認不認得他，因為他知道系統會幫他簽到。

en.content:
Difficult customer: "Do you know who I am?"
Me, smiling: "No problem, sir.
We have a customer database I can look up.
Would you like to give me your membership number?"
Three seconds of silence: "...Never mind."
Turns out "who I am"
was a question he couldn't answer either.
en.editorNote: Send this to front-desk coworkers recently held hostage by "do you know who I am?" The customer database is this era's best entitled-customer detector — actual VIPs don't announce themselves; their number does it for them.
variations: Customers who ask "do you know who I am?" are usually the ones nobody had to remember.
Real VIPs don't ask if you recognize them. They know the system already checked them in.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Pleated Jeans 2026-05-25 「24 雙 Skechers 分九天到貨」 viral retail story; rewritten as 「客人把店員當宅配 + 個人購物秘書」 dual-role satire — original framing

zh-tw.content:
客人網購了二十四雙鞋，
分九天到貨。
每天我們打電話通知，他每天說「我很忙」。
第十天他終於來了，要求我們當場拆封檢查，
拆到一半說「我趕時間」就走了。
一週後他回來，要求我們把所有鞋子
用紙箱重新打包成他要的尺寸。
先生，您買的是鞋子，
不是把我們當您的私人物流加管家。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給賣場、超商收包裹的同事——客人總以為一筆消費就買斷了店員的時間表。我們收貨、通知、保管、拆封、重新打包，每一項都有實際成本，公司沒收，客人不付，最後變成員工的隱形工時。
variations: 您家樓下有便利商店，您家裡有 PChome 24h，怎麼就是覺得我們店是您的私人倉庫？
客人下單一秒，員工服務九天——這個時薪換算，全台灣沒有公司付得起。

en.content:
She ordered 24 pairs of shoes online.
They arrived over nine days.
We called every day. She said "I'm busy" every day.
Day ten, she finally showed up, demanded we unbox every pair for inspection,
then halfway through said "I'm in a rush" and walked out.
A week later she came back and asked us to repackage everything
into custom-sized boxes she wanted.
Ma'am, you bought shoes —
not a private logistics team plus a personal assistant.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone in retail or convenience-store parcel handling. Customers act like one purchase buys our entire calendar. Receiving, notifying, storing, unboxing, repackaging — every step has a real cost, the company doesn't charge for it, the customer doesn't pay for it, and it all turns into invisible employee overtime.
variations: You have a convenience store downstairs and Amazon Prime at home. Why is our store your personal warehouse?
One second to click "buy." Nine days of staff time to serve. No hourly wage on earth covers that math.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 報導者 2026 餐飲缺工率 28% 報導；rewritten as 「四家店缺一家，那一家剛好就是我」 statistical-fatigue satire — original framing

zh-tw.content:
新聞說：餐飲業缺工率 28%，
每四家店就有一家在缺人。
我心想——
那一家剛好就是我這家。
隔壁那家也是。
再隔壁那家也是。
原來 28% 不是統計，
是公司故意算給投資人看的下限。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「我們是團隊」洗腦、結果一個人撐三人份的同事。報告上的缺工率永遠比你眼前的低——因為你眼前那家，就是被當作分母來平均的。
variations: 報告說每四家缺一家，現場感覺每家都缺三個人。
缺工率 28% 是給老闆看的版本，現場版本叫做「今天又只有我一個」。

en.content:
The news says: food-service labor shortage is at 28%.
One in four stores is short-staffed.
I think to myself —
that one is mine.
Next door's also short.
The one after that, also short.
Turns out 28% isn't a statistic.
It's the floor the company shows investors.
en.editorNote: Send to any coworker drinking the "we're a team" Kool-Aid while doing the work of three. The shortage rate on the report is always lower than the one in front of you — because the store in front of you is being averaged into the denominator.
variations: The report says one in four. The floor feels like three short everywhere.
28% is the version for the boss. The floor version is called "it's just me again today."

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 工作板 「服務至上但只能忍氣吞聲？」 thread; rewritten as 「公司的『以客為尊』翻譯成『以員工為墊背』」 word-substitution joke — original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
公司的 SOP 第一頁寫著：
「以客為尊」。
翻譯成現場版：
以客人為尊，
以員工為墊背，
以老闆為決策，
以薪水為最後一名。
四個排序裡只有一個跟員工有關，
剛好是最後那一個。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合貼在員工休息室、或印在跟主管討論調薪時的小抄上。SOP 的順序就是公司的價值觀順序——員工排在客人後面、排在老闆後面、排在帳本後面。
variations: 公司的優先順序：客人第一、老闆第二、KPI 第三、員工排在「以上皆是」後面。
「以客為尊」這四個字，公司省下的是員工四個字的尊嚴。

en.content:
Page one of the company SOP says:
"Customer first."
Floor translation:
customer first,
employee as cushion,
boss makes the call,
wage finishes last.
Four rankings. Only one involves the employee —
and it's the last one.
en.editorNote: Tape it in the break room or stash it in your notes for your next raise conversation. The order of the SOP is the order of the company's values — employees rank below customers, below the boss, below the ledger.
variations: Corporate priorities: customer first, boss second, KPI third, employee right after "all of the above."
"Customer first" is two words the company saves by spending two words of employee dignity.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 方格子 《以為有錢就了不起的客人請默默找個地方去死》 review; rewritten as 「有錢買的是商品，買不到我陪您演苦情劇」 transactional-limit framing — original anchor

zh-tw.content:
「我有付錢欸。」
是的，您付了錢。
所以您有那杯咖啡、那份餐、那張收據。
您沒有買到的是——
我陪您演苦情劇的時間、
我裝沒聽到您罵髒話的耐心、
我下班後還在想您那句話的失眠夜。
那些不在發票上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「我有付錢」開頭壓過的同事。錢買的東西在收據上，沒買的東西在你心裡——而後者通常比前者貴很多。
variations: 您的發票上列出來的，是您買到的東西；沒列出來的，是您沒買到的東西。員工的耐心剛好在第二類。
「我有付錢」這句話的潛台詞，就是「我以為錢買得到所有東西」——很可惜，沒有。

en.content:
"I'm paying, you know."
Yes. You paid.
So you got the coffee, the meal, the receipt.
What you didn't buy —
my time co-starring in your tragedy,
my patience pretending I didn't hear the cursing,
my sleepless night replaying that thing you said.
None of that is on the receipt.
en.editorNote: Send to any coworker recently crushed by the "but I'm paying" opener. What money buys is on the receipt. What it doesn't buy lives in your head — and that part usually costs much more.
variations: What's on your receipt is what you bought. What's not on it is what you didn't. Employee patience falls in the second category.
"I'm paying" subtext: "I assumed money buys everything." Sorry — it doesn't.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by TwistedSifter 2026-03 supermarket-layout-rage viral story; rewritten as 「客人罵的不是改版，是失控感」 psychological-reframe — original framing

zh-tw.content:
客人為了賣場動線改了、
醬油從第三排移到第七排，
當場罵了我十分鐘。
他罵的不是醬油，
他罵的是這個世界又有一件事不在他控制之中——
而我，剛好是那個世界派來的人。
他要的不是道歉，
是把世界調回他熟悉的樣子。
那個我做不到，我只能默默把醬油送他到第七排。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「店為什麼要改」、「東西為什麼換位置」開頭轟炸過的賣場同事。客人罵的從來不是商品，是他自己對失控的恐懼——我們只是剛好被選中的代表。
variations: 客人罵的不是改版的賣場，是改版的人生；只是人生不在我們的 SOP 裡。
醬油換位置就罵十分鐘的人，通常其他事情也沒有他想像中那麼可控。

en.content:
A customer found out we'd moved the soy sauce
from aisle three to aisle seven
and yelled at me for ten minutes.
He wasn't yelling about soy sauce.
He was yelling about one more thing in the world he can't control —
and I happened to be the world's representative on duty.
He didn't want an apology.
He wanted the world set back the way he remembered it.
That one's not in my power. I just walked him over to aisle seven.
en.editorNote: Send to any supermarket coworker who got chewed out over a remodel or a relocation today. The customer was never yelling at the product — they were yelling at their fear of losing control, and we just happen to be the day's chosen delegate.
variations: He wasn't yelling at the remodeled store. He was yelling at a remodeled life. The remodel isn't on our SOP.
Anyone who yells for ten minutes about soy sauce moving aisles is usually finding everything else just as unmanageable.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the recurring Dcard 工作板 「我跟你們老闆很熟」 name-drop trope; rewritten as a 「真的很熟就不用報名號」 logic-flip — original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
客人：「我跟你們老闆很熟喔。」
我：「那真是太好了，
那您要不要直接打給他，
請他幫您破例就好？」
客人：「⋯⋯我沒有他電話。」
原來「很熟」的定義，
是熟到連電話都沒存。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「我跟你們老闆很熟」威脅過的同事。真正跟老闆熟的客人，從來不需要對著基層店員報名號——他直接走後門就好。
variations: 會搬出「我跟你們老闆很熟」的人，通常跟老闆的關係，就跟我跟金城武一樣熟。
真的跟老闆熟，會打給老闆；跟老闆不熟，才會威脅店員。

en.content:
Customer: "I know your boss really well, you know."
Me: "Oh, wonderful.
Why don't you just call him
and have him make the exception for you?"
Customer: "...I don't have his number."
Turns out "really well"
means not well enough to save the contact.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone threatened with "I know your boss" this week. Customers who actually know the boss never name-drop to a frontline clerk — they just go around the back.
variations: People who pull out "I know your boss really well" usually know the boss about as well as I know Keanu Reeves.
If you actually knew the boss, you'd call the boss. You only threaten the clerk when you don't.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 心情板 久坐不點餐 / 占位 trope; rewritten as 「客人把咖啡廳當共享辦公室」 modern-remote-work satire — original framing

zh-tw.content:
客人點一杯最便宜的美式，
坐了五個小時。
中間借了三次插座、
問了兩次 Wi-Fi 密碼、
開了一場視訊會議、
還請我幫他把冷氣調強一點。
先生，您買的是一杯咖啡，
不是月租的共享辦公室。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「一杯飲料坐整天」客人占位到翻桌率歸零的咖啡廳同事。我們很歡迎您來，只是這裡是咖啡廳，不是您家書房，也不是您公司沒付的那間會議室。
variations: 點一杯美式坐五小時，這不是消費，這是入住。
咖啡廳的座位是用來喝咖啡的，不是用來辦您整間公司的線上會議。

en.content:
He ordered the cheapest Americano
and sat for five hours.
Borrowed an outlet three times,
asked for the Wi-Fi password twice,
ran a video call,
and asked me to crank up the AC.
Sir, you bought a coffee —
not a monthly membership at a co-working space.
en.editorNote: Send to any cafe coworker whose table turnover hit zero because of the "one drink, all day" guest. You're welcome here — it's just that this is a cafe, not your home office, and not the conference room your company forgot to rent.
variations: One Americano, five hours in the seat. That's not a purchase, that's a check-in.
Cafe seats are for drinking coffee, not for hosting your entire company's video calls.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the recurring 「你這什麼態度」 attitude-accusation trope on Dcard 工作板; rewritten as a mirror-flip where the customer's own tone is the answer — original phrasing

zh-tw.content:
客人一進門就摔東西、罵髒話、
用手指著我的鼻子，
然後問我：「你這什麼態度？」
我心裡想：
我的態度，是照著您的態度調出來的。
您給我十分的火，
我還能回您一個微笑，
這已經是我這份薪水做得到的極限。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被「你這什麼態度」三個字壓過的同事。我們的態度從來不是憑空生出來的，是客人遞過來、我們努力接住的那一份——而我們接得，已經比公司付的薪水多太多了。
variations: 客人問「你這什麼態度」的時候，最該照鏡子的，通常是問問題的那個人。
您給的是十分的火，我回的是一個微笑，這個匯率，您其實賺很大。

en.content:
He walked in slamming things, swearing,
pointing a finger in my face,
then asked: "What's with your attitude?"
What I thought:
my attitude is calibrated to match yours.
You hand me a ten out of ten of rage,
and I still hand back a smile —
that's already the limit of what this wage can do.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone flattened by "what's with your attitude?" today. Our attitude never comes from nowhere — it's the one the customer handed over and we tried to catch. And we catch far more than the wage pays for.
variations: When a customer asks "what's with your attitude," the person who most needs a mirror is usually the one asking.
You gave me a ten of rage and I gave back a smile. Honestly, on that exchange rate, you came out ahead.

items.sourceNote: Original warm-solidarity closer extending the file's 互助 register; the 「你今天接住了多少人」 reframe of invisible emotional caretaking is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
今天你笑著幫一個慌張的客人找到他要的東西，
你耐著性子重複講了第三次怎麼操作，
你在最忙的時候還是輕聲說了「不好意思讓您久等」。
沒有人替這些事鼓掌，
但你今天，
默默接住了好多個別人的壞心情。
下班了，
換我請你，把自己也接住。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合下班後傳給服務業的朋友，或自己存著。我們整天在接住別人，常常忘了自己也需要被接住一次——今天就先從對自己溫柔開始。
variations: 你今天接住了那麼多人的情緒，記得也留一隻手接住你自己。
沒有人替服務業鼓掌，那就讓我們自己人，替彼此拍一下手。

en.content:
Today you smiled and helped a flustered customer find what they needed.
You patiently explained how it works for the third time.
Even at your busiest, you still said softly, "Sorry to keep you waiting."
Nobody claps for any of that,
but today, quietly,
you caught so many other people's bad moods before they hit the floor.
Now you're off the clock —
let me catch you for a change.
en.editorNote: Good to send a service-industry friend after a shift, or to save for yourself. We spend all day catching other people and forget we need catching too — so start tonight by being gentle with yourself.
variations: You caught so many people's feelings today. Keep one hand free to catch your own.
Nobody claps for service workers, so let's clap for each other.

items.sourceNote: Extends the Dcard 「奧客經典語錄」 thread — the 「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題」 deflection line; rewritten with a fresh boomerang framing where the customer's own logic returns to them

zh-tw.content:
客人最愛說：「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題。」
奇怪了，東西是您買的、規則是您沒看的、
時間是您自己拖的，
怎麼最後變成只有「我們的問題」？
您這句話的重量，全壓在我一個人的肩膀上，
而我連喊一聲累，都得先說「不好意思」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被「那是你們的問題」這六個字一句話打發掉的同事。客人把問題丟過來的速度，永遠比他承認問題的速度快。
variations: 客人說「那是你們的問題」的時候，通常是在把自己的問題快遞給你，運費還要你出。
所有事情到最後都變成「你們的問題」，那請問我的薪水，是不是也算我自己的問題？

en.content:
The customer's favorite line: "That's your problem, not mine."
Strange — you bought it, you skipped the rules,
you're the one who left it till the last minute,
yet somehow it all becomes "our problem"?
The full weight of that sentence lands on my shoulders alone,
and I can't even say I'm tired without first saying "sorry."
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker brushed off today with "that's your problem." Customers always hand off a problem faster than they'll ever admit owning one.
variations: When a customer says "that's your problem," they're really shipping you their problem — postage due, paid by you.
If everything eventually becomes "your problem," then I guess my paycheck is my problem too?

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the 2026 retail-workforce framing of frontline staff as 「human shock absorbers」 absorbing customer frustration; original 「人體避震器」 vehicle-part metaphor is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
公司說我是第一線。
其實我是人體避震器——
客人路上塞車的氣、被老闆罵的氣、
跟家人吵架的氣，
全都顛到我這裡，由我吸收。
避震器壞了會換新的，
我壞了，公司只說：「下一位。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天替全世界的壞情緒墊背、回家整個人散架的同行。我們不是不會壞，是壞了沒人換、只能自己慢慢修。
variations: 服務業是整台車最便宜、最常壞、卻最不被感謝的那個零件。
客人帶來的不是消費，是他從進門前累積的一整天，由我替他接地。

en.content:
The company calls me the front line.
Really I'm a human shock absorber —
the traffic that cut them off, the boss who chewed them out,
the fight they had at home,
every bump lands on me, and I soak it up.
A worn shock absorber gets replaced.
When I wear out, the company just says: "Next."
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who cushions the whole world's bad moods all day and goes home rattling apart. It's not that we don't break — it's that nobody swaps us out, so we patch ourselves up slowly.
variations: Service workers are the cheapest, most replaced, least thanked part on the whole vehicle.
Customers don't bring you a transaction. They bring you the entire day they had before walking in.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「I don't get paid enough for this」 viral-thread format; original 「這個薪水沒包含這一項」 itemized-receipt framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
有些時刻，心裡只會冒出一句話：
「這個薪水，沒有包含這一項。」
被吼、被丟錢、被指著鼻子、
被當成出氣筒——
這些都是公司沒寫進職務說明、
也沒算進時薪裡的隱藏項目。
我領的是基本工資，做的卻是全餐。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合做完一件遠超職責範圍的爛事之後，私訊給最懂你的同事。我們不是不願意做，是這份薪水真的沒買到這麼多。
variations: 服務業的薪水算的是有寫在職缺上的，沒寫的那些，全是佛心免費附贈。
「我這個薪水沒包含被你罵」這句話，我每天在心裡刷三次卡。

en.content:
Some moments, only one line comes to mind:
"This wage does not cover this."
Getting yelled at, having money thrown at me, a finger in my face,
being somebody's punching bag —
none of it is in the job description,
none of it is priced into the hourly rate.
I'm paid for the basics and delivering the full course.
en.editorNote: Good to DM the coworker who gets you, right after you handle something miles outside your job. It's not that we won't do it — this paycheck genuinely didn't buy all that.
variations: The wage covers what's written in the listing. Everything else is a free gift from the goodness of our hearts.
"This pay doesn't include being yelled at by you" — I swipe that card three times a day in my head.

items.sourceNote: Extends the 方格子 「客人欺善怕惡」 register with the 「說話一定要大聲」 volume-as-power trope; original 「音量不等於道理」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
有些客人有個共同的信仰：
講話越大聲，道理就越站得住腳。
他在櫃檯前吼得整間店都聽得到，
以為音量是一種談判籌碼。
其實全店的人只記得一件事——
那位很大聲的客人，
沒有人記得他到底有沒有理。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人用音量壓著打、卻沒辦法回嘴的同事。大聲的人贏的只有當下，輸的是全店對他的印象。
variations: 音量不等於道理，吼得越大聲，通常只代表他手上的牌越爛。
客人以為提高音量是加分，其實全店都在心裡幫他扣分。

en.content:
Some customers share one belief:
the louder you talk, the more right you are.
He yells loud enough for the whole store to hear,
as if volume were a bargaining chip.
But everyone in the store only remembers one thing —
the really loud guy.
Nobody remembers whether he actually had a point.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got steamrolled by sheer volume today with no way to answer back. The loud one wins the moment and loses the whole room's opinion.
variations: Volume isn't an argument. The louder someone gets, the worse the cards in their hand usually are.
He thinks turning up the volume scores points. The whole store is quietly docking them.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「都是他做的，我什麼都不知道」 buck-passing 厭世職場語錄 from the manager/colleague angle into a service-floor scene; original 「最後一線」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
出事的時候，主管說：「我什麼都不知道，是他們現場處理的。」
客訴的時候，公司說：「這是員工個人行為。」
業績好的時候，功勞是團隊的；
出包的時候，責任是我一個人的。
站在最前線的意思是：
好處輪不到我先拿，黑鍋永遠我先背。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近替主管擔了不該擔的責任、卻連一句謝謝都沒收到的同行。最前線聽起來很威風，其實是最先中槍的位置。
variations: 好事是團隊的，壞事是我的，這種數學服務業每天都在算。
站第一線不是榮譽，是出事時離砲口最近的那個位置。

en.content:
When something goes wrong, the manager says: "I had no idea, the floor staff handled it."
When there's a complaint, the company says: "That was the employee's personal conduct."
Good numbers? Credit goes to the team.
A screwup? The blame is mine alone.
Being on the front line means:
I'm never first to the reward, always first to take the fall.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who carried blame that wasn't theirs for a manager lately and didn't even get a thank-you. "Front line" sounds heroic — it's really just the spot that gets hit first.
variations: Good news belongs to the team, bad news belongs to me. Service workers do that math every day.
Front line isn't an honor. It's the position closest to the muzzle when something goes off.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the canonical 「條碼刷不過 = 我猜是免費的吧」 retail in-joke from Keeping's 2026 customer-service jokes roundup; original 「全世界都覺得這句很好笑，只有店員笑不出來」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
商品條碼刷三次都嗶不過，
客人一定會說那句：「那是不是免費的啊？哈哈。」
我也一定會笑。
但這個笑話，全世界覺得好笑，
只有當天第七次聽到的我，笑不出來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又被同一個梗笑話招呼的櫃檯同事。客人以為自己很幽默，其實這句話我們一天要陪笑十次。
variations: 客人以為自己是第一個說「免費的吧」的人，其實他是今天的第七個。
服務業最會演的不是道歉，是把聽過一百次的爛笑話陪笑成第一次。

en.content:
The barcode won't scan after three tries,
and the customer always says it: "Guess it's free then, huh?"
And I always laugh.
The whole world finds that joke funny —
just not the version of me hearing it for the seventh time today.
en.editorNote: Send to the cashier coworker greeted by the same joke again today. The customer thinks they're hilarious; we laugh at this one ten times a shift.
variations: The customer thinks they're the first to say "guess it's free." They're actually number seven today.
The best acting in service work isn't the apology. It's laughing at a hundredth-time joke like it's the first.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「retail is hide-and-seek, the customer always finds you」 meme from Pleated-Jeans' 2026 customer-service collection; original 躲廁所 / 補貨間 hiding-spot escalation is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
服務業是一場永遠輸的捉迷藏。
我躲進補貨間，客人找得到。
我躲去倉庫盤點，客人找得到。
我連躲進廁所，門外都有人喊：「請問有人嗎？」
這份工作最神奇的是——
我隨時都能被找到，只有薪水永遠找不到。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每次想喘口氣就被客人精準定位的同行。我們不是不想休息，是這份工作沒有真正的隱身點。
variations: 服務業的躲貓貓只有客人會贏，因為他們有雷達，我們只有制服。
店員可以躲到任何地方，唯獨躲不掉下一個喊「不好意思」的人。

en.content:
Service work is a game of hide-and-seek you always lose.
I hide in the stockroom — they find me.
I hide in the warehouse doing inventory — they find me.
I hide in the bathroom and someone knocks: "Anyone in there?"
The magic of this job:
I can be found anytime — only the paycheck stays hidden.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who gets pinpointed the second they try to catch a breath. It's not that we don't want a break — this job has no real hiding spot.
variations: In service-work hide-and-seek only the customer wins. They have radar; we have a uniform.
A clerk can hide anywhere except from the next person about to say "excuse me."

items.sourceNote: Reframes the canonical 「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題」 奧客 deflection line from the Dcard 心情板 奧客語錄 thread; original 「問題會搬家」 ownership-transfer framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人最強的句型：「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題。」
一句話，問題就從他那邊，瞬間搬到我這邊。
是他輸錯密碼、是他沒帶會員卡、是他自己看錯日期，
但只要這句一出，
所有的錯，就都變成我站著該扛的。
服務業最累的不是做事，是替別人收養他不要的問題。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人一句話甩鍋、還得笑著善後的同行。問題會搬家，而且永遠往制服那邊搬。
variations: 客人說「這是你們的問題」的瞬間，問題的戶籍就遷到我名下了。
服務業收的不只是錢，還有一堆客人不肯認領的錯。

en.content:
The customer's most powerful line: "That's your problem, not mine."
One sentence, and the problem teleports from their side to mine.
He typed the wrong password, forgot his card, misread the date —
but the moment he says it,
every mistake becomes the one I'm standing here to carry.
The tiring part of service work isn't the tasks. It's adopting the problems other people refuse to claim.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who got a problem dumped on them in one sentence today and had to clean it up smiling. Problems relocate — always toward whoever's wearing the uniform.
variations: The instant a customer says "that's your problem," the problem changes its legal address to mine.
Service work collects more than money. It collects every mistake customers won't claim.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「working hard or hardly working?」 tired-customer-joke trope from danfe.io's 2026 work-stress humor guide; original 「廢話偵測器」 small-talk-fatigue framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人最愛問的廢話前三名：
「很閒喔？」（我手上同時在做四件事）
「你們生意是不是不好？」（現在是離峰時段）
「上班很爽吧，吹冷氣坐著？」（我已經站八小時了）
我們不是不想回，
是回了你也不信，
不如把那口氣留著，撐到下班。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又被客人用「你很閒喔」開場的同行。我們的忙不忙，從來不是用客人眼睛看得到的方式在進行。
variations: 客人看到的「很閒」，是我們把忙亂藏在微笑底下的成果。
服務業的閒，是一種演技，不是一種狀態。

en.content:
The customer's top three pointless questions:
"Slow day, huh?" (I'm doing four things at once)
"Business not great?" (it's literally off-peak)
"Must be nice, just sitting in the AC?" (I've been standing for eight hours)
It's not that we don't want to answer.
It's that you won't believe us anyway —
so we save the breath to make it to clock-out.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone greeted with "slow day, huh?" again today. Whether we're busy was never something visible to the customer's eye.
variations: The "slow day" a customer sees is just how well we hid the chaos behind a smile.
In service work, looking idle is a performance, not a state.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 方格子 「客人欺善怕惡」 register through the 「叫你們店長出來」 manager-summoning escalation; original 「店長不是更高一階的我，是同一個被罵的人」 solidarity reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人說：「叫你們店長出來！」
好像店長是傳說中可以滿足所有願望的人。
但客人不知道，
店長出來，講的會是一模一樣的規定，
只是他被罵的時候，
還要分一半的眼神回頭安慰我。
我們不是上下級，
是同一條船上，輪流站在砲口前的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人吵著要找主管、結果店長一起被罵的同行。店長不是更高階的擋箭牌，是跟你一起淋雨的人。
variations: 客人以為店長是大魔王，其實店長只是另一個穿制服、規定背得比較熟的我。
「叫你們店長出來」換來的，通常是兩個人一起被罵，而不是一個人被救。

en.content:
The customer says: "Get me your manager!"
As if the manager were some legend who grants every wish.
What they don't know:
the manager comes out and quotes the exact same policy —
only while getting yelled at,
he still spares half a glance back to reassure me.
We aren't ranks above and below.
We're on the same boat, taking turns standing at the muzzle.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone whose customer demanded a manager today, only for the manager to get yelled at too. The manager isn't a higher-ranking shield — they're the person standing in the same rain as you.
variations: The customer thinks the manager is the final boss. The manager is just another me in a uniform who memorized the policy better.
"Get me your manager" usually buys two people getting yelled at, not one person getting rescued.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 心情板 「奧客經典語錄」 thread — 「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題」 line; original 「問題分配權」 ownership-redistribution framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人最強的一句：「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題。」
翻譯過來是：「我不想負責，但你要負責讓我滿意。」
奧客不是不懂責任，
是太懂了——
懂到知道怎麼把它整包塞給站在櫃檯的你。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天剛被「那是你們的問題」這句話打發掉的同行。責任沒有消失，只是被轉帳到你的情緒帳戶。
variations: 客人說「不是我的問題」的時候，通常已經把問題打包好，貼上你的名字了。
奧客的超能力，是把自己的麻煩瞬間變成你的責任。

en.content:
The customer's ultimate line: "That's your problem, not mine."
Translation: "I refuse to be responsible, but you're responsible for keeping me happy."
Difficult customers don't misunderstand responsibility.
They understand it perfectly —
well enough to pack the whole thing up and hand it to the person at the counter.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone brushed off with "that's your problem" today. The responsibility didn't vanish — it just got wired into your emotional account.
variations: When a customer says "not my problem," they've usually already packed the problem up with your name on the label.
A difficult customer's superpower is turning their own mess into your responsibility in one sentence.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Modern Health 2026 retail-workforce data (can't close a laptop and walk away); original 「沒有關機鍵」 always-on framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
辦公室的人壓力大，可以闔上筆電走人。
我們的壓力，是站在原地、面對下一個排隊的人。
沒有暫停鍵，沒有關機鍵，
只有下一聲「下一位」。
服務業的累，不是做完就結束，
是做完還得馬上補位給下一個。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給覺得「我又不是坐辦公室，怎麼會比較累」被質疑過的同行。累的不是體力，是沒有縫隙的連續性。
variations: 別人下班是關機，我們下班是強制斷電——電量早就見底了。
辦公室的壓力會結束，櫃檯的壓力只會換一個人重來。

en.content:
Office workers can close the laptop and walk away.
Our stress is standing still, facing the next person in line.
No pause button. No power-off.
Just the next "next, please."
Service-industry tired doesn't end when the task ends.
It ends, then immediately refills for the next one.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who's been told "you don't even sit at a desk, how are you tired?" The exhaustion isn't physical — it's the gapless, back-to-back continuity.
variations: Other people clock out by shutting down. We clock out by force-quitting — the battery hit zero hours ago.
Office stress finishes. Counter stress just resets with a new face.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by TVBS 「14種奇葩奧客」 同行心聲 piece; original 「奧客圖鑑」 collector-card framing turning daily abuse into a wry catalogue is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
做服務業久了，會收集到一整本奧客圖鑑：
結帳前甜、結帳後酸的變臉款；
打烊才進門的關門款；
「我朋友比較便宜」的查無此人款。
別人收集公仔，我們收集陰影，
而且還是非自願的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給做久了已經能一眼分類奧客的資深同行。我們的職業病，是把傷害整理成圖鑑才笑得出來。
variations: 服務業的成就系統，是集滿各種奧客類型，獎勵是更厚的黑眼圈。
別人的收藏會增值，我們的奧客圖鑑只會增加離職的念頭。

en.editorNote: Send to the veteran who can sort a difficult customer at a glance. Our occupational hazard is cataloguing the damage just to be able to laugh at it.
variations: Service work's achievement system: collect every customer type. The reward is darker eye circles.
Other people's collections appreciate in value. Our customer bestiary only grows the urge to quit.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 換日線 「款待精神被無限上綱」 critique; original 「以客為尊 ≠ 以客為神」 one-character escalation is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
公司說「以客為尊」，
客人聽成「以客為神」。
尊重是雙向的，崇拜是單向的——
我可以尊重你，
但我沒有義務在你面前燒香。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合做成員工休息室的小卡。以客為尊是服務的底線，不是要員工把客人供起來拜。
variations: 把客人當尊貴的人是專業，把客人當神來拜是失去自己。
以客為尊的「尊」是尊重，不是要員工跪著尊敬。

en.content:
The company says "honor the customer."
The customer hears "worship the customer."
Respect runs both ways. Worship runs one.
I can respect you —
I'm just not obligated to burn incense in front of you.
en.editorNote: Good as a break-room card. Honoring the customer is the baseline of service, not a command to build them a shrine.
variations: Treating a customer as a valued person is professional. Treating them as a deity is losing yourself.
The "honor" in "honor the customer" means respect — not kneeling.

items.sourceNote: Reframes Modern Health 2026 finding that burnout is systemic not personal; original 「不是你抗壓性低，是缺工把三個人的班壓在你身上」 understaffing-reframe is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
你不是抗壓性低。
是這間店本來該排三個人的班，
現在只剩你一個在撐。
累到想哭不是你脆弱，
是一個人扛了三個人的重量，
還被問為什麼撐不住。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近因為缺工被排成一打三、又被主管說「現在年輕人吃不了苦」的同行。撐不住不是你的問題，是排班表的問題。
variations: 缺工不是你的能力問題，是公司把省下來的人事費，換成你的黑眼圈。
一個人做三個人的事，累的時候別怪自己，要怪那兩個沒被補上的缺。

en.content:
You're not weak under pressure.
This store was supposed to schedule three people for this shift.
Now it's just you, holding the floor.
Wanting to cry from exhaustion isn't fragility.
It's carrying three people's weight
and still being asked why you can't keep up.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone scheduled one-against-three by understaffing, then told "young people just can't handle hard work." Not coping isn't your problem — it's the schedule's.
variations: Understaffing isn't a skills gap. It's the company trading the payroll it saved for the dark circles under your eyes.
Doing three people's jobs alone — when you're exhausted, don't blame yourself. Blame the two empty slots nobody filled.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 心情板 「奧客經典語錄」 thread; the 「這是你們的問題不是我的問題」 deflection line rewritten as a one-way ownership transfer is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人最愛說：「這是你們的問題，不是我的問題。」
奇怪的是，
錯的時候永遠是我們的問題，
對的時候卻又變成他的功勞。
原來『問題』也是看心情分配的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天剛被推鍋的同事。出包了是「你們的問題」，順利了是「我早就說過」，這套邏輯客人練得比我們的 SOP 還熟。
variations: 客人的「不是我的問題」，翻譯成中文是：我不想負責，但我想罵人。
錯的歸我們，對的歸他——這不是服務，是替客人保管所有他不想要的責任。

en.content:
The customer's favorite line: "That's your problem, not mine."
Funny thing —
when it goes wrong, it's always our problem.
When it goes right, it's suddenly their credit.
Turns out "problem" gets handed out based on mood too.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got the blame pinned on them today. Mess up and it's "your problem"; smooth sailing and it's "I told you so." Customers practice that logic harder than we practice our SOP.
variations: "Not my problem" translates to: I don't want to take responsibility, but I do want to yell.
We get the failures, they get the wins — that's not service, it's storing every responsibility the customer doesn't want.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the 2026-05 TwistedSifter story on a worker used as a customer's emotional punching bag; the 「我的工作是賣商品，不是收你的情緒垃圾」 boundary line is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人站在櫃檯前，
把他今天所有的不順，
一句一句倒在我身上。
我想說的是：
我這裡賣的是商品，
不是您情緒的回收站。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被客人當垃圾桶倒情緒、回家整個人空掉的同行。聽客人說話是工作，被當情緒回收站不是。
variations: 客人付的錢買的是杯子，不是把店員當情緒垃圾分類的入場券。
我的耳朵是用來確認您的餐點，不是用來承接您整個禮拜的怨氣。

en.content:
The customer stands at the counter
and pours out everything that went wrong in their day,
one sentence at a time, onto me.
What I want to say is:
I sell products here,
not recycling space for your emotions.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone used as a customer's emotional dumpster lately, who goes home feeling hollowed out. Listening to customers is the job; being their emotional recycling bin isn't.
variations: Their money bought a cup, not an admission ticket to sort their feelings into the clerk.
My ears are here to confirm your order, not to catch a whole week of your resentment.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Modern Health 2026 finding that 40% of retail workers report declining mental health; original 「下班後的我，是白天那個我的二手版本」 secondhand-self image is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
報告說，每十個服務業的人，
就有四個覺得自己的心理狀態在往下掉。
我懂——
白天那個會笑、會應對、會說「好的沒問題」的我，
下班後只剩一個二手版本回家，
而且還是過了保固的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近覺得自己越來越笑不出來的同行。狀態下滑不是你想太多，是四成的人跟你一起在下滑——你只是其中一個誠實說出來的。
variations: 服務業最累的不是站著，是把今天最好的自己留給了客人，剩下的才帶回家。
白天的我是新品，下班的我是展示品，回到家只剩瑕疵品特價出清。

en.content:
The report says four in ten service workers
feel their mental health slipping.
I get it —
the me who smiles, handles things, says "sure, no problem" all day
goes home as a secondhand version of myself.
Warranty already expired.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who's been finding it harder to smile lately. The decline isn't you overthinking — four in ten are sliding right alongside you. You're just one of the honest ones.
variations: The exhausting part isn't standing all day. It's giving the best version of yourself to customers and bringing home what's left.
Morning me is brand new, off-shift me is the floor model, home me is the clearance-rack reject.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 奧客語錄 「叫你們店長出來」 manager-summoning trope; original 「店長出來也是人，不是您的人形出氣筒升級版」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人怒吼：「叫你們店長出來！」
店長出來了，
客人以為自己升級了。
其實只是換一個人，
陪您把同一段不講理，
再聽一次。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人喊「叫店長出來」、後來發現店長也只能跟你一起苦笑的同事。換人聽不會讓無理變有理，只會多累一個人。
variations: 「叫店長出來」不會讓您比較對，只會讓兩個人一起記住您今天的樣子。
客人以為升級客訴對象就會贏，其實只是把不講理重播一次給更累的人聽。

en.content:
The customer roars: "Get me your manager!"
The manager comes out,
and the customer thinks they've leveled up.
Really, it's just a different person
who gets to hear the same unreasonable rant
one more time.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got hit with "get me your manager" today and watched the manager end up sharing the same tired smile. Swapping listeners doesn't make a bad point good — it just exhausts one more person.
variations: "Get me your manager" doesn't make you more right. It just means two people now remember how you acted today.
Escalating the complaint doesn't win anything — it just replays the unreasonableness for someone even more tired.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the canonical 「掃不過就喊『那是不是免費』」 overused-customer-joke trope from Keeping's 2026 customer-service-jokes roundup; original 「這個梗的保固，在我入行第一週就過期了」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
商品掃不過去，客人一定會說：
「那是不是免費的？」
然後自己笑得很開心。
這個梗我每天聽八次，
它的保固期，在我入行第一週就過了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天被同一個『免費梗』突襲、還得配合笑的櫃檯同事。客人以為是首發，其實是當日第八場重播。
variations: 客人覺得自己很幽默的那一刻，通常是服務人員一天裡最需要演技的一刻。
「掃不過是不是免費」這句話，好笑程度跟它出現的頻率成反比。

en.content:
When something won't scan, the customer always says:
"Guess it's free, then!"
and cracks up at their own line.
I hear it eight times a day.
Its warranty expired in my first week on the job.
en.editorNote: Send to the cashier who gets ambushed by the same "it's free" joke daily and still has to laugh along. The customer thinks it's a premiere — it's the eighth rerun of the day.
variations: The moment a customer thinks they're hilarious is usually the moment a service worker needs their best acting.
How funny "guess it's free" is — inversely proportional to how often it gets said.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「retail customer service is hide-and-seek, except the customers always find you」 meme from Keeping 2026; original 補貨躲在貨架後仍被找到 visual is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
服務業是一場永遠的捉迷藏，
只是規則反過來——
躲的人是我，找的人是客人，
而客人永遠贏。
就算我蹲在最裡面那排貨架後面補貨，
也會有一隻手伸過來：「小姐，這個有沒有大一號的？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給以為躲到倉庫、躲到後場就能喘口氣，結果還是被精準定位的零售同行。我們不是員工，是會走路的搜尋引擎。
variations: 服務業的躲，從來沒成功過——客人有雷達，而且只對店員開機。
你以為補貨是逃生，客人卻覺得那是你最方便被問問題的位置。

en.content:
Service work is an endless game of hide-and-seek,
except the rules are flipped:
I hide, the customer seeks,
and the customer always wins.
Even crouched behind the very back shelf restocking,
a hand still reaches in: "Miss, got this in a bigger size?"
en.editorNote: Send to the retail friend who thought ducking into the stockroom meant a breather, then got pinpointed anyway. We're not employees — we're walking search engines.
variations: Hiding never works in service. Customers have radar, and it only switches on for staff.
You think restocking is an escape. The customer thinks it's the most convenient spot to ask you a question.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「customer service runs on passive aggression and deep breathing」 meme from SurveySparrow 2026; original 「深呼吸是我們的職業病，也是職業技能」 dual-framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人問我怎麼還能這麼冷靜，
我說我不是冷靜，
我是在深呼吸。
服務業靠兩樣東西運轉：
一點點不能說出口的不爽，
跟很多很多次的吸氣、吐氣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給上班時把『生氣』改成『深呼吸』度過一整天的同行。我們不是 EQ 高，是練到肺活量比 EQ 大。
variations: 服務業的冷靜不是修養，是把每一句想罵的話換算成一次深呼吸的結果。
客人看到的是我面不改色，沒看到的是我已經默默吐氣到第三十次。

en.content:
A customer asked how I stay so calm.
I'm not calm.
I'm just breathing.
Service work runs on two things:
a little passive aggression I can't say out loud,
and a whole lot of inhale, exhale.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got through the whole day by converting "anger" into "deep breathing." It's not high EQ — it's lung capacity bigger than our patience.
variations: Service-industry calm isn't composure. It's every comeback I swallowed, cashed in for one more deep breath.
The customer sees a straight face. They don't see me silently on my thirtieth exhale.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 2026 奧客語錄 thread observation that customers can file complaints but employees have no equivalent channel; original 「客訴有專線，奧客沒有檢舉鍵」 asymmetry framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人不爽，有客訴專線、有意見箱、有「叫你們店長出來」。
我不爽，有的只是——
一個轉身、一個深呼吸，
還有把臉重新調回微笑的那兩秒。
這場遊戲從來不公平：
他們有檢舉鍵，我們只有靜音鍵。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被奧客投訴、卻連一句反駁都不能說的同行。不公平的不是被罵，是被罵的人連申訴的地方都沒有。
variations: 客人有一百種管道表達不滿，員工只有一種：忍。
客訴信寄得出去，員工的委屈卻只能寄放在心裡，還沒有回執。

en.content:
When a customer's upset, there's a complaint hotline, a suggestion box, a "get me your manager."
When I'm upset, all I get is
a turn of the back, a deep breath,
and the two seconds it takes to reset my face to a smile.
The game was never fair:
they get a report button, we get a mute button.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got complained about today and couldn't say a word back. The unfair part isn't being yelled at — it's that the one being yelled at has nowhere to file a grievance.
variations: Customers have a hundred ways to express displeasure. Employees have one: endure.
The complaint email gets sent. The employee's hurt just gets stored — no receipt issued.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「working hard or hardly working?」 overused-customer-line trope from Keeping 2026; original 「這句話本身就是我今天最累的工作」 self-referential framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人走過來，笑笑地說：
「忙啊？還是在偷懶啊？」
我也笑笑地回：「忙啊。」
沒講出口的是——
回答這句話本身，
就是我今天最累的一份工作。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給被客人用『工作或偷懶』這種自以為幽默開場過的同行。我們不是不會接話，是接這種話比搬貨還累。
variations: 客人覺得最輕鬆的一句玩笑，常常是員工當天最需要硬撐的一場演出。
「在偷懶喔？」——問這句的人，從來沒站過我們站的這八小時。

en.content:
A customer strolls up, grinning:
"Working hard, or hardly working?"
I grin back: "Working hard."
What I don't say is —
answering that line
is the hardest work I'll do all day.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone opened on with the "working hard or hardly working" bit. It's not that we can't volley back — it's that volleying back is heavier than hauling boxes.
variations: The joke a customer finds easiest is often the performance an employee has to grit through hardest.
"Slacking off, huh?" — the people who ask have never stood the eight hours we just stood.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Help Scout 「customer service is hide-and-seek」 joke; original 「躲哪都被找到」 visibility-curse framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
服務業就像在玩躲貓貓，
差別只在於——
不管我躲到倉庫、廁所、還是後門抽風口，
需要找麻煩的客人，
永遠第一個找到我。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一進倉庫補貨、客人就準時出現喊『有人嗎』的同事。我們不是隱形，是專門被雷達鎖定的那種顯眼。
variations: 服務業沒有放空時間，因為你一放空，客人的雷達就響了。
想休息五分鐘，宇宙就會派一個客人來提醒你——你還在上班。

en.content:
Service work is basically hide-and-seek,
with one twist:
no matter where I hide — stockroom, bathroom, back-door fan vent —
the customer who needs something
always finds me first.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who steps into the stockroom and a customer instantly appears yelling "anybody here?" We're not invisible — we're the kind of visible that's permanently radar-locked.
variations: There's no zoning out in service. The second you do, the customer radar goes off.
Try to take a five-minute break and the universe sends a customer to remind you you're still on shift.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「run on passive aggression and deep breathing」 funny quote; original 「員工燃料表」 fuel-gauge framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
服務業員工不需要咖啡，
我們靠兩種燃料就能上一整天班：
壓在喉嚨裡的那句髒話，
還有一口比一口深的深呼吸。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天靠深呼吸撐到下班的同事。我們不是脾氣好，是把脾氣換算成氧氣在燒。
variations: 服務業的續航力，靠的不是熱情，是吞回去的那幾百句話。
別人上班喝咖啡提神，我們上班深呼吸保命。

en.content:
Service workers don't run on coffee.
We run on two fuels for the whole shift:
the curse word stuck in our throat,
and breaths that get deeper by the hour.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who deep-breathed their way to clock-out today. It's not that we have a good temper — we just convert it into oxygen and burn it.
variations: Service-industry stamina isn't passion. It's the few hundred things we swallowed.
Other people drink coffee to stay sharp. We deep-breathe to stay alive.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Keeping 「I guess it's free!」 won't-scan customer-line trope; original 「掃不出來不等於免費」 logic-gap framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
商品條碼掃不出來，
客人馬上眼睛一亮：「那是不是免費的？」
我笑著說：「不是喔。」
心裡想：「掃不出來的是條碼，
又不是您要付的錢。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又被『掃不出來就免費』這個老梗逗到笑不出來的櫃檯同事。這句話從你上班第一天就不好笑了，但客人講得永遠很有自信。
variations: 「掃不出來是不是免費」——這句話的笑點，早在我入職第一天就過期了。
條碼掃不出來，客人比我還開心，彷彿中了發票一樣。

en.content:
The barcode won't scan,
and the customer's eyes light up: "Guess it's free, then?"
I smile: "Nope."
Inside I'm thinking: "What didn't scan is the barcode —
not the money you still owe."
en.editorNote: Send to the cashier coworker who couldn't fake a laugh at the "guess it's free" bit again today. That line stopped being funny on day one of the job, yet customers keep delivering it with full confidence.
variations: "Guess it's free?" — that joke expired on my very first day on the register.
When the barcode won't scan, the customer's happier than I am, like they just won the lottery.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 奧客語錄 「叫你們店長出來」 escalation trope; original 「店長也只是另一件比較貴的制服」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人氣到喊：「叫你們店長出來！」
店長出來後，講的跟我一字不差。
客人愣了三秒——
原來他要的不是答案，
是一個職稱比較大、可以讓他贏的對象。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人『跳級喊話』、結果店長講一樣的話的同事。客人要的常常不是解決，是一個能讓他覺得自己贏了的人。
variations: 「叫店長出來」的客人，要的不是更高的答案，是更高的階級可以踩。
店長講的跟我一樣，客人就突然安靜了——可見問題從來不是那句話。

en.content:
The customer snaps: "Get me your manager!"
The manager comes out and says exactly what I said, word for word.
The customer freezes for three seconds —
turns out they didn't want an answer.
They wanted a bigger job title to beat.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got escalated past today, only for the manager to repeat them verbatim. Customers often don't want a solution — they want someone who lets them feel like they won.
variations: The "get me your manager" customer doesn't want a higher answer. They want a higher rank to step on.
The manager said the same thing I did and the customer went quiet — proof the line was never the problem.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「support team coping with humor」 burnout-meme register; original 「下班後的笑跟上班時的笑不是同一種」 two-smiles framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
上班時的笑，是發給客人的。
下班後跟同事在後巷邊抽菸邊罵的笑，
才是發給自己的。
服務業最珍貴的福利，
不是員工價，是有人陪你一起苦中作樂。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個下班後願意陪你站在後門邊吐槽客人的戰友。撐過服務業的，從來不是熱情，是身邊那群一起笑著罵的人。
variations: 上班的笑是商品，下班跟同事的笑才是退貨後拿回來的真心。
服務業最能回血的時刻，是收店後大家一起把今天的奧客笑著演一遍。

en.content:
The smile on shift is issued to customers.
The smile after clock-out — out back, ranting with a coworker —
that one's issued to myself.
The best perk in service work
isn't the staff discount. It's someone to laugh through the misery with.
en.editorNote: Send to the work buddy who'll stand out by the back door after close and roast the day's customers with you. What gets people through service work was never passion — it's the crew who laughs and complains alongside you.
variations: The on-shift smile is merchandise. The after-shift laugh with a coworker is the sincerity you got back as a refund.
The best recovery moment in service work is the whole crew reenacting today's worst customer, laughing, after lockup.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「10大奧客經典語錄」 list (我老客戶誒 / 別間可以你們怎不可); original 「老客戶會員卡只存在他自己腦裡」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人說：「我可是你們老客戶誒。」
我心裡想：「您的會員卡，
只存在您自己的記憶裡，
而且每次刷，折扣都自動加倍。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天被「我老客戶」開頭壓折扣的櫃檯同事。老客戶三個字是奧客的萬用通行證，可惜系統永遠查無此卡。
variations: 「我老客戶」是不用印的會員卡，效期永久、折扣無上限，只是查無資料。
客人的『老客戶』年資，通常比這間店開的時間還久。

en.content:
Customer: "You know I'm a regular here."
Me, internally: "Your loyalty card
exists only in your own memory —
and every swipe somehow doubles the discount."
en.editorNote: Send to the front-counter coworker getting leveraged for discounts with "I'm a regular" all day. It's the all-purpose pass — too bad the system never finds the card.
variations: "I'm a regular" is an unprinted loyalty card: never expires, unlimited discount, no record found.
The customer's years as a "regular" often predate the day the store opened.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「it's free / it won't scan」 retail meme; original 「掃不到 ≠ 免費」 register rewritten as a tired-cashier monologue

zh-tw.content:
東西掃不到的時候，
客人一定會說：「那是不是免費的？」
這個笑話，
我從上班第一天聽到現在，
它沒變好笑，只變老了，跟我一起。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天都要陪客人演一次「掃不到等於免費」的收銀同事。有些笑話客人講得很有自信，只有我們知道它已經死了一千次。
variations: 「掃不到是不是免費」這句，全台灣的收銀機每天被講三萬次，沒有一次成功。
客人以為掃不到很好笑，其實掃不到只代表我要手動輸入一長串條碼。

en.content:
Whenever something won't scan,
the customer always says: "Guess it's free, then?"
I've heard that joke
every day since my first shift.
It hasn't gotten funnier. It's just gotten older — along with me.
en.editorNote: Send to the cashier who has to fake-laugh at "guess it's free" daily. Some jokes the customer delivers with full confidence, while only we know they died a thousand deaths ago.
variations: "Guess it's free" gets said thirty thousand times a day at registers everywhere, and it has never once worked.
The customer thinks a failed scan is hilarious. To me it just means typing in a very long barcode by hand.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「有限消費、無限服務」 critique from the 換日線 service-culture register; original 「以客為尊被翻譯成以客為神」 mistranslation framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
「以客為尊」這四個字，
不知道哪一年被偷偷翻譯成「以客為神」。
尊重是雙向的，
當神是單向的——
而我們，從來沒有報名要當廟公。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合貼在員工休息室，提醒大家界線在哪。尊重客人沒問題，把客人供成神就是另一回事了。
variations: 以客為尊跟以客為神，差一個字，差的是我要不要燒香。
客人值得被尊重，但尊重不等於我得在櫃檯後面替您點光明燈。

en.content:
Somewhere along the way,
"the customer comes first" got quietly rewritten as "the customer is a deity."
Respect runs both ways.
Worship runs only one —
and none of us signed up to be the temple keeper.
en.editorNote: Good for the break room as a boundary reminder. Respecting customers is fine; enshrining them as gods is something else entirely.
variations: "Customer first" and "customer worship" are one word apart — and one of them requires incense.
Customers deserve respect. Respect doesn't mean I light a prayer candle for you behind the counter.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 奧客語錄 「那是你們的問題不是我的問題」 deflection line; original 「問題的所有權」 boundary framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人最愛的句子：「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題。」
奇怪的是，
每次出問題的源頭都是他，
問題的所有權卻永遠登記在我名下。
服務業最不公平的地方，
是錯由客人犯，鍋由員工背。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天剛被「那是你們的問題」甩鍋、回家還在生氣的同事。你不是難相處，你只是不想替別人的選擇道歉。
variations: 客人犯的錯，過戶速度比房子還快，三秒就登記到員工名下。
「那是你們的問題」翻譯成白話：請你用你的下班時間，處理我的不負責任。

en.content:
The customer's favorite line: "That's your problem, not mine."
Funny thing is,
the problem always starts with them,
yet the deed to it gets filed under my name.
The deepest unfairness in service work:
the customer makes the mistake, the employee carries the blame.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who got handed the "that's your problem" deflection today and is still fuming at home. You're not difficult — you just don't want to apologize for someone else's choice.
variations: A customer's mistake transfers ownership faster than a house — three seconds and it's filed under the employee.
"That's your problem" translates to: please use your off-hours to clean up my irresponsibility.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 2026 「customer service: therapy, unpaid edition」 pun register; original 「免費心理諮商所」 framing rewritten for the Taiwanese service context

zh-tw.content:
我的職稱是櫃檯人員，
實際業務是：免費心理諮商所、
客人婚姻問題傾訴窗口、
以及陌生人壞心情的回收站。
諮商所收費一小時兩千，
我收費——時薪一百九。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天被客人當情緒諮商師、卻沒拿到諮商師薪水的同行。我們聽的故事不比心理師少，只是沒人替我們開帳單。
variations: 服務業是全台灣收費最便宜的心理諮商所，諮商師還要負責結帳。
客人把整週的委屈倒給我，倒完心情好了，換我帶著他的情緒下班。

en.content:
My job title says front-desk clerk.
My actual duties: free therapy clinic,
intake window for customers' marriage problems,
and recycling bin for strangers' bad moods.
A real therapist charges a hundred an hour.
I charge — minimum wage.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone treated as a free counselor by customers without the counselor's pay. We hear no fewer stories than a therapist — nobody just sends us the bill.
variations: Service work is the cheapest therapy clinic in town, and the therapist also has to ring you up.
The customer dumps a whole week of grievances on me, feels better, and leaves — and I clock out carrying their mood home.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Dcard 「奧客經典語錄」 thread's 「我可以客訴你，你不能拿我怎樣」 asymmetry; original 「客訴是單行道」 one-way-street framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
奧客最大的底氣是：
「我可以客訴你，你不能拿我怎樣。」
客訴是一條單行道，
車全往我這邊開，
而我連按喇叭的權利都沒有。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被「信不信我客訴你」威脅過的同行。客訴制度保護的是誰，站過櫃檯的人最清楚。
variations: 客人手上那張客訴單，是把單面刀，刀口永遠朝著員工。
真正該被立法保護的，是讓員工也能客訴奧客的那一天。

en.content:
A difficult customer's ultimate trump card:
"I can report you. You can't do a thing about me."
Complaints are a one-way street —
every car drives toward me,
and I don't even get to honk.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone threatened with "want me to file a complaint?" this week. Who the complaint system actually protects — the people behind the counter know best.
variations: That complaint form in the customer's hand is a single-edged blade, and the edge always faces the employee.
The law we actually need is the one that lets employees file complaints against the customers.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the TwistedSifter 2026 「emotional punching bag」 service-worker piece; original 「情緒沙包」 boxing-bag metaphor rewritten for the counter context

zh-tw.content:
客人來買的是一杯咖啡，
順便把今天的氣、這週的怨、
還有跟老闆吵架的火，
全打在我臉上。
我不是櫃檯，是一個會說「歡迎光臨」的情緒沙包。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人當出氣筒、回家臉還在隱隱作痛的同行。被打的是沙包，痛的是裡面那個人。
variations: 客人把整天的壞情緒往我這裡倒，倒完輕鬆了，重量全留給我背回家。
情緒沙包也是會破的，只是公司以為它無限耐打。

en.content:
The customer came in for a coffee
and threw in today's anger, this week's resentment,
and the fight with their boss —
all straight at my face.
I'm not a counter. I'm a punching bag that says "welcome in."
en.editorNote: Send to anyone used as a vent today, going home with a face that still aches. The bag takes the hit — the person inside takes the pain.
variations: The customer dumps a whole day of bad mood on me, walks out lighter, and leaves the weight for me to carry home.
Even a punching bag splits eventually. The company just assumes it takes infinite hits.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the CTWANT 2026 民生消費 53萬人缺口 labor-shortage report; original 「一個人當三個人用，薪水還是一個人的」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
缺工潮的真相是：
店裡少了兩個人，
那兩份工作沒有消失，
只是默默搬到了我的班表上。
一個人當三個人用，
薪水條上卻只有一個人的數字。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近一個人撐整間店、累到懷疑人生的同行。缺的不是工，是願意被當三個人用、卻只領一份錢的人。
variations: 公司說缺工，其實沒缺，缺的人都長在我身上多出來的那兩雙手。
缺工潮裡最忙的不是老闆，是那個被迫一人分飾三角的員工。

en.content:
The truth about the labor shortage:
two coworkers are gone,
but their two jobs didn't disappear —
they quietly migrated onto my schedule.
One person doing the work of three,
while the paycheck still shows one person's number.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone running a whole store solo and questioning their life choices. It's not a labor shortage — it's a shortage of people willing to do three jobs for one wage.
variations: The company says we're understaffed. We're not — the missing staff grew as two extra hands on me.
The busiest person in a labor shortage isn't the boss. It's the employee forced to play three roles at once.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 2026 「Smile.exe running / On mute, still judging」 customer-service pun register; original 「微笑是開機自動執行的程式」 OS-process metaphor is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
我的微笑是開機自動執行的程式，
上班打卡那一刻就在背景跑，
關不掉、刪不掉、佔記憶體，
下班想結束它，
系統還會問我：「確定要關閉嗎？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給下班後笑容還卡在臉上、自己都嫌僵的同行。職業微笑不是表情，是常駐後台的程式。
variations: 服務業的微笑像防毒軟體，明明很吃資源，公司卻規定不准關。
我的臉開了一個叫「微笑」的分頁，八小時都沒辦法按 X。

en.content:
My smile is a program that auto-launches on startup.
The second I clock in, it's running in the background —
can't close it, can't delete it, eats all my memory.
At clock-out I try to end it,
and the system asks: "Are you sure you want to quit?"
en.editorNote: Send to anyone whose smile is still stuck on their face after the shift, stiff enough to notice. The work smile isn't an expression — it's a resident background process.
variations: The service smile is like antivirus software: it hogs resources, but the company won't let you disable it.
My face has a tab open called "Smile," and for eight hours there's no X button.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 方格子 「客人欺善怕惡」 piece with a fresh 錄影威脅 angle from 2026 search results; original 「鏡頭一舉，我就從人變成證據」 framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人吵不贏的時候，手機就舉起來：
「我錄起來囉，傳上網讓大家評評理。」
那一刻我不再是一個人，
是一段隨時會被剪輯、配音、下標的證據。
我的飯碗，握在他三十秒的影片裡。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給最近被客人舉手機威脅、嚇到不敢多說一句的同行。鏡頭面前最沒有防備的，永遠是那個照規定做事的人。
variations: 客人錄的不是真相，是只剪我那半邊的版本。
手機一舉，員工就從活人變成隨時被公審的素材。

en.content:
The moment a customer can't win the argument, the phone goes up:
"I'm recording this — let the internet decide."
Right then I stop being a person
and become footage to be cut, captioned, and dubbed at will.
My livelihood, held inside their thirty-second clip.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone scared into silence by a customer raising their phone. In front of the lens, the most defenseless person is always the one just following the rules.
variations: What the customer records isn't the truth — it's the version edited down to only my half.
Phone goes up, and the employee turns from a living person into material for a public trial.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 2026 新竹早午餐 老闆娘護員工事件 (「不用說對不起」, 401.5萬觀看) covered by 經理人/udn/ETtoday; original framing of the boss stepping in front of the staff member is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
店員嚇到說出「對不起」的那一秒，
老闆娘擋到她前面：
「不用說對不起，你又沒做錯。」
那句話比加薪還珍貴——
原來真的有人會把員工
擋在自己的背後。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給遇到挺員工老闆的同行，也傳給還在獨自承受奧客的人，告訴他：好老闆不是傳說。被擋在背後的那一刻，比業績漂亮多了。
variations: 員工最想聽到的不是「客人最大」，是老闆那句「我罩你」。
會擋在員工前面的老闆，留住的不只是人，是整個店的士氣。

en.content:
The second the clerk flinched and said "I'm sorry,"
the owner stepped in front of her:
"Don't apologize. You did nothing wrong."
That one line was worth more than a raise —
turns out someone really will
put their employee behind their own back.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone with a boss who has their back, and to anyone still facing difficult customers alone — proof that good bosses aren't a myth. Being shielded beats a pretty sales chart.
variations: What workers most want to hear isn't "the customer comes first." It's the boss saying "I've got you."
A boss who steps in front of their staff keeps more than the staff — they keep the whole store's morale.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 新竹奧客「妳是在審犯人嗎」 line from the 2026 早午餐事件; original 「點個餐而已，我又不是嫌犯」 interrogation-flip framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人吼：「妳是在審犯人嗎？不能讓我看完菜單？」
我心裡想：「我只是問您要不要加蛋，
您卻把點餐演成偵訊室。
真要說審問，被三句話就嚇到道歉的，
從來都是我。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給點個餐就被當成在刁難客人的外場同事。問句變罪狀，只因為對方今天心情不好——這不是你的問題。
variations: 我只是問您要不要加蛋，不是在做筆錄。
點餐被嫌像審問的，永遠是員工；真正在審判別人的，是那個摔菜單的人。

en.content:
Customer: "Are you interrogating a suspect? Can't you let me read the menu?"
Me, internally: "I just asked if you wanted to add an egg,
and you turned ordering into an interrogation room.
If anyone's on trial here, it's me —
the one who flinches and apologizes after three sentences."
en.editorNote: Send to the floor staff treated like they're badgering customers just for taking an order. A question becomes an accusation only because someone's having a bad day — not your fault.
variations: I asked if you wanted an egg, not for your statement on the record.
The one accused of "interrogating" is always the employee. The one actually putting someone on trial is the person throwing the menu.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the udn 職場觀測 report that the 新竹早午餐店 saw 業績暴增5成 after the owner protected staff; original 「網友用五星投票決定誰是對的」 public-verdict framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
以前我們以為，得罪客人就是得罪生意。
直到那家店挺了員工，業績反而漲五成。
原來真正會回頭的客人，
不是被你跪出來的，
是看見你連員工都捨得保護，
才決定把錢花在這裡。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給還在糾結「奧客也是客」的老闆，也傳給以為自己只能忍的員工。保護員工不是成本，是最划算的口碑投資。
variations: 得罪一個奧客，換來一群為你按五星的客人，這筆帳其實很好算。
客人選的不是最低聲下氣的店，是最有骨氣、連員工都護的那一家。

en.content:
We used to think offending a customer meant losing the business.
Then that store stood up for its staff — and sales jumped fifty percent.
Turns out the customers who actually come back
aren't the ones you knelt for.
They're the ones who saw you protect even your employees,
and decided to spend their money there.
en.editorNote: Send to the boss still agonizing over "a difficult customer is still a customer," and to the worker who thinks enduring is the only option. Protecting staff isn't a cost — it's the best word-of-mouth investment going.
variations: Offend one difficult customer, gain a crowd leaving you five stars — the math actually works out.
Customers don't pick the most groveling store. They pick the one with a spine that shields its own staff.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 寰宇新聞 「奧客壓垮服務業？50萬職缺沒人想上」 2026 segment; original 「不是沒人想做，是沒人想被當沙包做」 labor-shortage cause-flip framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
新聞說：服務業五十萬職缺沒人想上。
標題寫「年輕人吃不了苦」。
沒寫的是：
不是沒人想做，
是沒人想花八小時，
被當成可以隨便大小聲的沙包做。
缺的不是工，是尊重。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給被長輩唸「現在年輕人都不肯做服務業」的人。請他看完整個句子：缺工的真正原因，從來不在員工身上。
variations: 五十萬職缺沒人填，不是大家懶，是大家終於學會不拿尊嚴換時薪。
服務業缺的不是人手，是一個不用被罵也能好好上班的環境。

en.content:
The news says: half a million service jobs and no takers.
The headline reads "young people can't handle hard work."
What it leaves out:
it's not that no one wants the job —
it's that no one wants to spend eight hours
being a punching bag anyone can yell at.
It's not a labor shortage. It's a respect shortage.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone nagged with "young people won't do service work anymore." Ask them to read the whole sentence — the real cause of the shortage was never the workers.
variations: Half a million unfilled jobs isn't laziness. It's people finally refusing to trade their dignity for an hourly wage.
Service work isn't short on hands. It's short on a workplace where you can clock in without getting yelled at.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the TVBS 「14種奇葩奧客 同行直喊根本我心聲」 2026 list; original 「奧客的要求自相矛盾」 contradiction-as-physics-defying framing is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
客人：「奶茶不要太冰、不要太熱、不要太溫。」
我：「……所以您要的是常溫但冰的熱奶茶？」
客人：「對，很難懂嗎？」
難的不是奶茶，是物理。
而我，只是一個被要求違反物理定律的店員。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天剛接到「三個不要」訂單、當場大腦當機的飲料店同行。客人不是奧，是把員工當成可以推翻熱力學的魔術師。
variations: 客人的要求不是高，是同時要冰山跟火山住在同一杯裡。
點一杯違反物理定律的飲料，然後怪店員聽不懂——這就是奧客的日常魔法。

en.content:
Customer: "Milk tea — not too cold, not too hot, not too lukewarm."
Me: "...So you want a room-temperature iced hot milk tea?"
Customer: "Yes. Is that so hard to understand?"
The milk tea isn't hard. Physics is.
And I'm just a clerk being asked to break the laws of thermodynamics.
en.editorNote: Send to the drink-shop coworker whose brain blue-screened on a "three nots" order today. The customer isn't difficult — they think the employee is a magician who can overrule thermodynamics.
variations: It's not a tall order. It's wanting an iceberg and a volcano to share the same cup.
Order a drink that defies physics, then blame the clerk for not getting it — that's the everyday magic of a difficult customer.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the PTT WomenTalk 「服務業的禮貌只是防客訴」 thread — the insight that 'welcome' is a defense mechanism, not hospitality, is the rewrite core

zh-tw.content:
你以為我說「歡迎光臨」是熱情。
不，那是我在快速判斷你會不會客訴。
微笑是盾牌，不是心情。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天進門前先深呼吸、把脾氣鎖進置物櫃的第一線同事。重點不是冷漠，是那份禮貌其實是保命用的。
variations: 我們的禮貌不是教養好，是被客訴教出來的反射動作。

en.content:
You think my "Welcome!" is warmth.
It's not — it's me speed-calculating whether you'll file a complaint.
The smile is a shield, not a mood.
en.editorNote: Send to the frontline coworker who takes a deep breath before every shift and locks their temper in the locker. The point isn't coldness — that politeness is a survival reflex.
variations: Our manners aren't good breeding. They're a reflex trained by complaint forms.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 工作板 「服務至上，但真的只能忍氣吞聲嗎？」 thread — the 'limited spend, unlimited service' expectation is the rewrite anchor

zh-tw.content:
你花的是一杯飲料的錢，
要的是一整個人的尊嚴。
抱歉，這裡不找零這種。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又被「我是顧客我最大」壓在地上摩擦的朋友。服務有價，但人的底線不在菜單上。
variations: 服務是工作，不是賣身契；你買得起商品，買不起我整個人。

en.content:
You paid for one drink
and expect a whole person's dignity.
Sorry, we don't make change for that.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who got ground into the floor by "the customer is always right" again today. Service has a price, but a person's bottom line isn't on the menu.
variations: Service is a job, not a deed of sale. You can afford the product, not the whole human.

items.sourceNote: Reframes NOWnews 「客服怒列3大奧客罪狀」 — the phone-customer who won't read anything and demands the agent recite it all is the rewrite scenario

zh-tw.content:
客人：「我懶得看，你直接念給我聽。」
於是我把三頁條款，用最溫柔的聲音，
一字一句念成你今天最後悔的決定。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給電話客服同事，尤其是剛被「你直接講重點」要求、結果客人聽到一半就喊煩的那種。客服的耐心是有極限的，只是聲音不會洩漏。
variations: 你叫我念，我就念到讓你想自己看。

en.content:
Customer: "I can't be bothered to read it. Just read it to me."
So I take all three pages of terms, in my gentlest voice,
and recite them into the worst decision of your day.
en.editorNote: Send to phone-support coworkers — especially the one who got hit with "just get to the point" and then the customer got annoyed halfway through. An agent's patience has limits; the voice just never gives it away.
variations: You asked me to read it. I'll read it until you wish you'd read it yourself.

items.sourceNote: Reframes Cheers 林承毅 essay on the emotional cost of mandatory smiling in service work — the gap between trained smile and real exhaustion is the rewrite core

zh-tw.content:
下班後我對著鏡子，發現自己還在笑。
不是開心，是臉忘了怎麼停。
原來情緒勞動，是連肌肉都要加班。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種下班後笑容卸不掉、回家對家人也不小心擺出營業臉的同事。提醒他：你可以把笑收起來，這裡沒有客人。
variations: 我的微笑是排班制的，只是下班那格忘了關。

en.content:
After my shift I look in the mirror and I'm still smiling.
Not from joy — my face forgot how to stop.
Turns out emotional labor makes even your muscles work overtime.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who can't peel the smile off after clocking out and accidentally gives family the customer-service face at home. Remind them: you can put it away now, no customers here.
variations: My smile is on a shift schedule. The clock-out slot just never got switched off.

items.sourceNote: Reframes a GirlStyle 厭世毒雞湯 listicle into a service-specific 'kindness shouldn't equal minimum wage' angle drawn from the Dcard job-board sentiment

zh-tw.content:
公司付我基本工資，
卻要我用全身的好脾氣去換。
親愛的，好脾氣不是免費贈品，是我硬擠出來的庫存。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給領著最低薪、卻被要求要有最高EQ的服務業朋友。好脾氣會用完，補貨還要自己付運費。
variations: 用基本工資買我的笑容，這筆交易我虧太多了。

en.content:
The company pays me minimum wage
and expects my entire reserve of patience in return.
Honey, good temper isn't a free gift with purchase — it's stock I have to squeeze out of myself.
en.editorNote: Send to the service friend earning the lowest pay but expected to have the highest EQ. Good temper runs out, and the restock shipping is on you.
variations: Buying my smile at minimum wage — I'm losing way too much on this deal.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the Dcard 'classic 奧客 lines' thread — specifically the 別家店都可以 trope — into a service worker's deadpan internal reply

zh-tw.content:
客人說：「別家店都可以，只有你們這裡不行。」
我笑著心想：那您怎麼沒去別家，是別家也婉拒您了嗎？
zh-tw.editorNote: 服務業最熟的開場白前三名，永遠是那個我們從沒見過的「別家店」。傳給每天被無形的別家店比下去的同事。
variations: 「別家店都可以」這句話的別家店，至今還是個傳說，沒人找得到。
您口中那間什麼都可以的別家店，建議您直接定居那裡。

en.content:
Customer: "Every other store lets me do this — only you guys won't."
Me, smiling: Then why aren't you at every other store? Did they turn you down too?
en.editorNote: Top three opening lines every service worker knows by heart, always starring that mythical 'every other store' none of us has ever seen. Send to the coworker who loses daily to an invisible competitor.
variations: That 'every other store' you keep mentioning is a legend — nobody has ever located it.
If that magical store lets you do anything, may I suggest you move in there permanently?

items.sourceNote: Reframes the Help Scout 'customer service is like being a superhero with a headset instead of a cape' line into a fresh self-deprecating angle without the cape cliché

zh-tw.content:
別人上班靠專業，
我上班靠演技。
每天戴上耳機那一秒，就是奧斯卡開拍。
zh-tw.editorNote: 客服的工作其實是表演藝術，只是沒有獎座也沒有掌聲，只有下一通電話。傳給那個聲音永遠很甜、私下很厭世的客服朋友。
variations: 我的工作內容：把『關你什麼事』翻譯成『非常感謝您的寶貴意見』。
耳機一戴，影帝上身；耳機一拿，瞬間斷電。

en.content:
Other people show up to work and use their skills.
I show up to work and use my acting range.
The second I put the headset on, the cameras start rolling.
en.editorNote: Customer service is performance art — just without the trophy, the applause, or the wrap party. Only the next call. Send to the friend whose phone voice is sugar and whose soul is dead inside.
variations: My actual job: translating 'none of your business' into 'thank you so much for that valuable feedback.'
Headset on, Oscar winner. Headset off, instantly unplugged.

items.sourceNote: Adapts the UDN 職場厭世語錄 listicle tone (餘額不足 / 準時下班需要勇氣) into a service-shift-specific closing-time lament

zh-tw.content:
都打烊了，
門口那位客人還在門縫裡跟我對眼神。
我們之間隔的不是玻璃，是我準時下班的全部勇氣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 打烊前五分鐘走進來的客人，是所有服務業共同的噩夢。傳給那個鐵門拉一半、心也涼一半的夥伴。
variations: 招牌燈關了，您的購物慾怎麼還亮著？
我們已打烊，但您的需求顯然還在營業中。

en.content:
We're closed,
yet there's a customer at the door, locking eyes with me through the gap.
What's between us isn't the glass — it's every ounce of courage I need to clock out on time.
en.editorNote: The customer who walks in five minutes before close is the shared nightmare of every service job. Send to the coworker whose shutter is half-down and whose hope is fully gone.
variations: The sign's off — why is your urge to shop still glowing?
We're closed, but your needs are apparently open 24 hours.

items.sourceNote: Rewrites the Thought Catalog 'you'd look better if you smiled / you'd look better if you showered' comeback into a cleaner, content-safe internal-monologue version

zh-tw.content:
客人說：「你笑一個會比較好看。」
我心想：您客氣一點，也會比較好相處，但我們都沒這麼做，對吧。
zh-tw.editorNote: 被陌生人命令『笑一個』的瞬間，是服務業最想翻白眼的時刻之一。微笑是服務，不是義務。傳給那個被要求微笑到臉抽筋的人。
variations: 我的笑容不是售後服務，恕不附贈。
您要的微笑，今天剛好賣完了。

en.content:
Customer: "You'd look prettier if you smiled."
Me, internally: You'd be easier to deal with if you were polite, but neither of us is doing that today, are we?
en.editorNote: Being ordered to 'smile' by a stranger is one of the all-time great eye-roll moments in service work. A smile is a service, not an obligation. Send to the one whose face is cramping from forced grins.
variations: My smile isn't an after-sales perk — sorry, not included.
The smile you ordered? Just sold out for today.

items.sourceNote: Rewrites the canonical Dcard 奧客語錄 'we have no record of this / 別家都可以只有你們不行' trope into a single internal-monologue beat

zh-tw.content:
客人：「別家都可以，只有你們這裡不行。」
我：「那您要不要去那家比較方便的？」
客人：「我就是要在你們這裡用。」
原來他要的不是服務，是表演舞台。
zh-tw.editorNote: 「別家都可以」是奧客的萬用開場白，通常後面那家店的名字永遠說不出來。傳給今天被這句話釘在櫃檯的同事，提醒他：那家店不存在，但你的下班時間存在。
variations: 他口中那家『什麼都可以』的店，地址是『不告訴你路』。
客人說別家都行，我說那很好啊，這裡是別家的隔壁。

en.content:
Customer: "Everywhere else lets me do this — only you guys won't."
Me: "Would the more convenient place suit you better, then?"
Customer: "No, I want to do it HERE."
Turns out they didn't want the service. They wanted a stage.
en.editorNote: "Everywhere else allows it" is the universal opener of difficult customers, and somehow the name of that other place never quite materializes. Send to the coworker pinned to the counter by this line today.
variations: That store where "anything goes"? Its address is "none of your business."
He said the other place allows it. Great — the other place is next door, go ahead.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the emotional-labor 'smile saved for customers, tears saved for after the shift' theme from Cheers + Dcard 工作板 into a quote

zh-tw.content:
上班八小時，我把笑容平均分給每一位客人。
下班那一刻，我才發現自己今天的份，一個都沒留。
zh-tw.editorNote: 情緒勞動最累的不是裝笑，是裝完之後發現沒人替你裝。寫給那個對所有人微笑，卻忘了對自己溫柔的人。
variations: 我的笑容是公司資產，回家才知道自己早就破產。
微笑是發給客人的，剩下的情緒才是我帶回家的行李。

en.content:
Eight hours on shift, I split my smile evenly among every customer.
Clocking out, I realized I'd saved exactly none of it for myself.
en.editorNote: The exhausting part of emotional labor isn't faking the smile — it's discovering, afterward, that no one was faking one back for you. For the person who's kind to everyone and forgets to be kind to themselves.
variations: My smile is a company asset; I only find out I'm bankrupt once I get home.
Smiles go to the customers. The leftover feelings are the luggage I carry home.

items.sourceNote: Rewrites the classic 'do you know who I am? / let me get someone who can help with that' retail comeback into a content-safe version

zh-tw.content:
客人拍桌：「你知道我是誰嗎？」
我冷靜拿起對講機：「保全請注意，這裡有位客人忘記自己是誰，需要協助。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 「你知道我是誰嗎」這句話的標準答案，就是讓他自己也想不起來。傳給那個今天被『大人物』威脅過的同事，這是想得到卻不能說出口的最佳回覆。
variations: 他問我知不知道他是誰，我說不知道，但我知道他下一站是出口。
您是誰我真的不清楚，但您再大聲，警衛就會幫您介紹自己。

en.content:
Customer slams the counter: "Do you know who I am?"
Me, calmly reaching for the intercom: "Security, we have a customer who's forgotten who he is and needs assistance."
en.editorNote: The correct answer to "do you know who I am?" is to make sure he can't quite remember either. Send to the coworker threatened by a self-declared VIP today — the comeback they wanted but couldn't say out loud.
variations: He asked if I knew who he was. No idea — but I know his next stop is the exit.
I genuinely don't know who you are, but get any louder and security will introduce you to yourself.

items.sourceNote: Adapts the 厭世職場語錄 'distance from vacation start to end' cynical-quote format to a service-shift context

zh-tw.content:
世界上最短的距離，是「我先休息一下」到「客人來了」。
世界上最長的距離，是現在到下班。
zh-tw.editorNote: 服務業的時間是彈性的：休息會瞬間蒸發，最後一小時會無限延長。傳給那個剛坐下就被門鈴叫起來的人。
variations: 椅子還沒坐熱，客人的問題已經煮滾。
我以為我在休息，其實只是站著發呆的進階版。

en.content:
The shortest distance in the world is from "let me just sit down for a sec" to "a customer walked in."
The longest distance in the world is from right now to clocking out.
en.editorNote: Time in service work is elastic: breaks evaporate instantly, the final hour stretches forever. For the one yanked back up by the door chime the second they sat down.
variations: The seat hasn't even warmed up before the customer's problem is at a rolling boil.
I thought I was on break. Turns out it was just standing-around-blankly, deluxe edition.

items.sourceNote: Rewrites the call-center '3大奧客罪狀 / crying after hanging up' theme into a phone-support internal monologue

zh-tw.content:
客人在電話那頭罵了十分鐘，最後說：「你聲音怎麼還這麼甜？」
因為我把音量留給您，把表情留給了靜音鍵後面那張快哭的臉。
zh-tw.editorNote: 客服最厲害的特技，是嘴角往上、眼眶往下，同時進行。傳給那個掛上電話才終於可以做表情的客服戰友。
variations: 電話這頭笑著說『為您服務』，靜音鍵那頭已經排練好嘆氣。
我的聲音很甜，是因為苦的部分都吞下去了。

en.content:
The customer yelled for ten minutes, then said: "How is your voice still so sweet?"
Because I gave you the volume and saved the real face for the one behind the mute button, the one about to cry.
en.editorNote: The greatest trick in call-center work is smiling with your mouth and welling up with your eyes at the same time. For the phone-support comrade who only gets to make a face once the call ends.
variations: "Happy to help!" on this end; a fully rehearsed sigh waiting behind the mute button.
My voice is sweet because I already swallowed all the bitter parts.

items.sourceNote: Rewrites the canonical 奧客語錄 '別家可以只有你們不行' threat-trope from Dcard 心情 board into a punchy two-liner

zh-tw.content:
「別家都可以，只有你們不行。」
「那您要不要去那家最可以的？」
「……我就是想看你們可不可以。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又被『別家都可以』威脅過的同事。重點從來不是別家可不可以，是想看你會不會破例。
variations: 客人說的『別家』，跟『我朋友』通常住在同一個查無此地的地址。
別家都可以？那您此刻為什麼站在我面前，不在別家。

en.content:
"Every other place lets me, just not you guys."
"Then maybe head to the place that lets you?"
"...I just want to see if you'll let me."
en.editorNote: Send to a coworker who got the "everywhere else allows it" threat again today. It was never about the other place — it's about whether you'll bend the rule.
variations: The customer's "every other place" lives at the same nonexistent address as their "friend."
Every other place lets you? Then why are you standing in front of me and not at one of them?

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 經理人 piece on rejection-sounding phrases ('這是公司規定' = wall-building); reframed as the employee's inner translation

zh-tw.content:
嘴巴說的是「不好意思，這是公司規定。」
心裡想的是「我也想幫您，但這條規定是用來保護我，不是用來氣您的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天得把『我做不到』翻譯成『公司規定』的第一線同事。那句話不是冷漠，是員工僅剩的盾牌。
variations: 『公司規定』四個字，是店員的盔甲，不是給客人的耳光。
我不是不想破例，是破了例之後要扛的人是我。

en.content:
What my mouth says: "Sorry, that's company policy."
What my head says: "I'd love to help, but this rule exists to protect me, not to spite you."
en.editorNote: For the frontline worker who spends all day translating "I can't" into "company policy." That line isn't coldness — it's the only shield the employee has left.
variations: "Company policy" is the clerk's armor, not a slap in the customer's face.
I'm not refusing to bend the rule — I'm the one who has to carry it after it breaks.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 518職場熊報 list on '爆單時的出餐速度客訴'; rewritten as kitchen/floor-staff time-pressure scene

zh-tw.content:
「怎麼這麼慢？我都等十分鐘了。」
您前面還有十二桌，每桌都覺得自己是第一個。
我們只有兩雙手，跟一個快被催到燒起來的鍋。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給爆單那天被催到懷疑人生的廚房和外場戰友。慢的不是手，是時間在尖峰時段特別黏。
variations: 客人覺得他等了十分鐘，鍋子覺得它連喘氣的十秒都沒有。
我不是不趕，是我同時在趕您和您前面那十二個您。

en.content:
"Why so slow? I've waited ten whole minutes."
There are twelve tables ahead of you, and every one of them thinks they're first.
We've got two pairs of hands and one pan about to catch fire from all the rushing.
en.editorNote: For the kitchen and floor crew who got rushed into an existential crisis on a packed night. It's not the hands that are slow — time just gets stickier at the dinner rush.
variations: The customer feels like they waited ten minutes; the pan feels like it didn't get ten seconds to breathe.
I'm not slow — I'm rushing you and the twelve other yous in line ahead of you, all at once.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the '客訴威脅' / '你的態度不好' theme (Dcard 台灣服務業 post + 經理人) into an internal monologue about smiling through complaints

zh-tw.content:
「你態度給我好一點，不然我客訴你。」
我態度已經是今天最好的版本了，
比較差的那個，正在心裡排隊等下班。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給被『我要客訴你』恐嚇過的服務業朋友。那一聲威脅，買走的不是你的錯，是你最後一點點不必微笑的權利。
variations: 客人說我態度差，其實是我把好態度全給了他，自己一份都沒留。
微笑是制服的一部分，下班才脫得下來。

en.content:
"Watch your attitude, or I'll file a complaint."
This is already the best version of my attitude today.
The worse one is lined up in my head, waiting for me to clock out.
en.editorNote: For the service worker who got the "I'll report you" threat. That threat doesn't buy your mistake — it buys away the last sliver of your right to not smile.
variations: They say my attitude is bad — really I gave all the good attitude to them and saved none for myself.
The smile is part of the uniform. I only get to take it off after my shift.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the canonical 「別家都可以，只有你們不行」奧客 line (Dcard 心情 奧客語錄 + 104職場力) into a comeback format

zh-tw.content:
「別家都可以，只有你們不行。」
「那您要不要去別家？」
「我就喜歡你們家。」
喜歡的方式很特別，是吧。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被「別家都可以」綁架的同事。對方嘴上嫌你不行，腳卻一步都不肯離開——你早就贏了，只是制服讓你不能笑出來。
variations: 「別家都可以」的別家，永遠是那間此刻不在現場、無法對質的店。
客人最忠誠的店，往往就是他罵得最兇的那一間。

en.content:
"Everywhere else lets me. Only you won't."
