"Want me to point you to everywhere else?"
"No, I like it here."
Funny way to show it.
en.editorNote: For the coworker held hostage by "everywhere else does it." Their mouth says you're useless, but their feet won't leave. You already won — the uniform just won't let you smile about it.
variations: "Everywhere else" is always the one store that isn't here right now to be asked.
The store a customer's most loyal to is usually the one they yell at the loudest.

items.sourceNote: Rewrites the viral retail trope of the un-scannable barcode → 「沒嗶到就是免費」 (Keeping 2026 customer service jokes list) as a service-worker monologue

zh-tw.content:
「嗶不過去？那這個是免費的囉！」
第一次聽覺得有點好笑，
第八百次聽，我笑的是您居然以為這梗還新。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給收銀台的戰友。同一句冷笑話，客人一輩子只講一次，店員卻一天要陪笑八次——辛苦你的臉了。
variations: 條碼嗶不過去不代表免費，只代表機器跟我一樣需要休息。
「沒嗶到就免費」這句話的保鮮期，大概是員工到職的第一個小時。

en.content:
"Won't scan? Guess it's free!"
The first time, mildly funny.
The eight-hundredth time, what's funny is that you think this line is new.
en.editorNote: For the comrade at the register. The customer tells that joke once in their life; the clerk has to fake-laugh at it eight times a shift. Your cheeks have been through a lot.
variations: A barcode that won't scan doesn't mean it's free — it means the scanner needs a break, same as me.
"It won't scan, so it's free" stays fresh for about the first hour of any retail job.

items.sourceNote: Reframes the 「我是老顧客欸」discount-entitlement trope (104職場力 客訴狀況 + Dcard 奧客語錄) into a quiet comeback

zh-tw.content:
「我可是老顧客欸，沒有優惠喔？」
老顧客我們真的很感謝，
只是感謝在心裡，不在收銀機裡。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給被「我是老顧客」要求打折的店員。常來是緣分，不是價目表——你不欠誰一個折扣，只欠自己一個準時下班。
variations: 常來的客人值得被記住，但記住不等於要在價格上認賠。
「我是老顧客」後面接的，通常不是感謝，是帳單。

en.content:
"I'm a regular here — no discount for me?"
We really are grateful for the regulars.
The gratitude just lives in our hearts, not in the register.
en.editorNote: For the clerk leaned on with "I'm a regular." Coming back often is a connection, not a price list — you don't owe anyone a discount, only yourself an on-time clock-out.
variations: Regulars deserve to be remembered — being remembered isn't the same as eating the markdown.
"I'm a regular" is usually followed not by a thank-you, but by an invoice.

items.sourceNote: Rewrites the worn 「上班很閒喔？」/ "working hard or hardly working?" greeting (Help Scout funny CS quotes + 2026 viral retail lists) into a service-worker comeback

zh-tw.content:
「站在這邊很閒喔？」
我站著的這八小時裡，
腳比您的耐心先沒電。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給被嫌「看起來很閒」的櫃檯戰友。看起來沒事，是因為我們把所有的累都藏進了笑容裡——那才是最費力的工。
variations: 看起來很閒的服務業，通常是把忙都藏在表情管理裡的那種。
我不是閒，我是把崩潰排程在下班之後而已。

en.content:
"Looks pretty easy just standing there, huh?"
In these eight hours of standing,
my feet run out of battery before your patience does.
en.editorNote: For the counter crew who got the "must be nice doing nothing" comment. We look idle because we've tucked all the exhaustion behind the smile — and that's the heaviest lifting of the day.
variations: Service work that looks easy is usually the kind hiding all the busy behind face management.
I'm not idle — I've just scheduled my breakdown for after my shift.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the 「叫你們主管出來」escalation trope (518職場熊報 無理客訴 + Dcard 奧客語錄) into an internal monologue

zh-tw.content:
「叫你們主管出來！」
主管出來，講的話跟我一模一樣，
只是您終於肯聽了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給被「叫主管出來」當成大絕的服務業朋友。同樣一句話，從你嘴裡是規定，從主管嘴裡才變成答案——委屈的不是道理，是說道理的人。
variations: 「叫主管出來」翻譯成白話，就是「我要聽到同一個答案，但語氣高一階」。
主管講完，客人點頭——原來缺的從來不是答案，是頭銜。

en.content:
"Get me your manager!"
The manager comes out and says exactly what I said.
The only difference is now you're willing to hear it.
en.editorNote: For the worker whose customer pulls "get me your manager" as a finishing move. Same words — from your mouth it's a policy, from the manager's it becomes the answer. The unfairness isn't the logic; it's who's allowed to say it.
variations: "Get me your manager" translates to "I want the same answer, but one rank louder."
The manager finishes, the customer nods — turns out what was missing was never the answer, just the title.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the 「我要客訴」threat-as-leverage trope (518職場熊報 + Dcard) into the asymmetry observation

zh-tw.content:
「我要客訴你！」
好喔，您有客訴的權利，
我有把今天記到日記裡的權利，
兩邊都沒人會看。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給被「我要客訴」當開場白問候的客服。客訴是客人的權利，扣薪是我們的代價——同一句話，兩邊的重量從來不一樣。
variations: 客人有客訴的權利，員工有「被客訴」的義務，這份合約沒人簽過卻人人遵守。
「我要客訴」翻譯成白話：我要一個你不能反駁我的位置。

en.content:
"I'm filing a complaint!"
Sure — you have the right to complain,
I have the right to write it in my diary,
and nobody reads either one.
en.editorNote: For the support agent greeted by "I'm filing a complaint" as an opener. The complaint is the customer's right; the pay deduction is our cost. Same sentence, two very different weights.
variations: Customers have the right to complain; employees have the duty to be complained about. Nobody signed that contract, but everybody honors it.
"I'm filing a complaint" translates to "I want a position where you can't talk back."

items.sourceNote: Reworks the 「掃不出來就是免費」scanner trope (retail worker memes, Help Scout funny CS quotes) with a fresh comeback

zh-tw.content:
「嗶不出來就是免費的吧？」
如果這個邏輯成立，
那我打卡機壞掉那天，是不是該領兩倍薪？
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又聽到「掃不出來算我賺到」這種冷笑話的零售同事。同一套「機器壞=我獲利」的邏輯，套在他們身上就理直氣壯，套回公司就立刻失效。
variations: 「嗶不出來免費」是客人版的薛丁格——商品同時免費又必須有貨。
機器掃不出來，免費的從來不是商品，是客人那一秒的幻想。

en.content:
"If it won't scan, it's free, right?"
If that logic holds,
then the day the time clock broke, do I get double pay?
en.editorNote: For the retail coworker who heard "won't scan, must be free" again today. The whole "machine broke = I profit" logic sounds airtight on them and dissolves instantly when pointed back at the company.
variations: "Won't scan, so it's free" is the customer's version of Schrödinger — the item is simultaneously free and definitely in stock.
When the scanner fails, the free thing was never the product — it was the customer's one-second fantasy.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the 「這是你們的問題不是我的」blame-deflection trope (Dcard 台灣服務業) into an internal monologue

zh-tw.content:
「這是你們的問題，不是我的。」
是是是，所以您現在站在我這邊的櫃台，
等我這邊的人，解決我這邊的問題。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給被「都是你們的問題」推到牆角的服務業朋友。錯歸誰永遠沒結論，但要誰收尾，客人心裡其實很清楚。
variations: 「這是你們的問題」後面，通常緊接著「所以你們要幫我解決」——責任和期待，從來不住同一個人身上。
客人把問題丟回給你的同時，也很誠實地把雙手攤開等你接住。

en.content:
"That's your problem, not mine."
Right, right — which is why you're standing at my counter,
waiting for my people,
to fix my problem.
en.editorNote: For the worker backed into a corner by "that's your problem." Who's at fault never gets settled — but who has to clean it up, the customer knows perfectly well.
variations: "That's your problem" is always followed by "so you'd better fix it for me" — blame and expectation never live in the same person.
The moment a customer tosses the problem back to you, they also very honestly hold out both hands for you to catch it.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the canonical 「別家都可以，只有你們不行」 cross-store comparison trope (Dcard 心情/奧客 forum) into a two-line punchline

zh-tw.content:
「別家都可以，只有你們不行。」
那您真的很適合別家，
我這邊幫您把門開好了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又被「別家都可以」綁架的服務業朋友。重點不是別家到底可不可以，而是對方從來沒打算去別家。
variations: 「別家都可以」是奧客的萬用咒語，唸完之後他還是站在你家門口不走。
如果別家真的都可以，那他現在應該在別家，而不是在你面前。

en.content:
"Every other store allows it. Only you don't."
Then those other stores sound perfect for you —
I've already got the door open.
en.editorNote: For the worker held hostage again by "every other store allows it." The point isn't whether the other stores actually allow it — it's that the customer was never going to go there.
variations: "Every other store allows it" is a difficult customer's magic spell — and after they cast it, they're still standing right in front of you.
If every other store really allowed it, they'd be at one of those stores right now, not glaring at you.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by NOWnews 客服怒列「3大奧客罪狀」 — the 「我認識你們老闆」 name-dropping intimidation trope, rewritten as a deadpan call-center exchange

zh-tw.content:
「我認識你們老闆喔。」
「太好了，那這件事您直接跟他講，
比我這邊快多了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給接電話接到「我認識你們老闆」的客服同事。真的認識的人不會用講的，會直接打給老闆。
variations: 「我認識你們老闆」翻譯成白話就是：我其實誰都不認識，但我希望你怕。
客人說認識老闆的時候，老闆通常正在會議室，連這通電話的存在都不知道。

en.content:
"I know your boss, you know."
"Great — then take this straight to him.
Honestly, it'll be way faster than going through me."
en.editorNote: For the call-center coworker who just heard "I know your boss." People who actually know the boss don't announce it — they just call the boss.
variations: "I know your boss" translates to: I actually know no one, but I'm hoping you'll be scared.
When a customer claims to know the boss, the boss is usually in a meeting, blissfully unaware this call exists.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by DQ 地球圖輯隊 explainer on 情緒勞動 (emotional labor / Hochschild); rewritten as a first-person clock-out monologue, not a definition

zh-tw.content:
下班打卡那一聲「嗶」，
不只是上班結束，
是我把今天借出去的那張笑臉，
終於還給自己。
zh-tw.editorNote: 寫給每天用微笑換薪水的人。情緒勞動最累的不是笑，是笑完之後要花力氣，才找得回原本的表情。
variations: 制服脫下來的時候，臉上那層客服模式也跟著掛回置物櫃。
下班的路上不是在發呆，是在把上班時關掉的那個自己，慢慢開機。

en.content:
That "beep" when I clock out
isn't just the end of my shift —
it's the moment I finally get back
the smile I lent out all day.
en.editorNote: For everyone who trades a smile for a paycheck. The exhausting part of emotional labor isn't the smiling — it's the effort it takes afterward to find your own face again.
variations: When the uniform comes off, that customer-service layer on my face gets hung back in the locker too.
On the commute home I'm not zoning out — I'm slowly rebooting the self I had to switch off at work.

items.sourceNote: Sparked by Dcard 調查局 piece on 服務業被丟錢/只能道歉/低薪 — reworks the 「丟錢結帳」 disrespect trope into a quiet dignity line

zh-tw.content:
錢丟在櫃台上會滾，
我彎下腰去撿的不是那枚硬幣，
是我提醒自己——
薪水可以低，腰不能塌。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給被客人把錢甩在桌上的服務業朋友。撿起來的是錢，撿不回來的是對方本來該有的那點基本尊重。
variations: 客人把錢丟在桌上，丟掉的其實是他自己的家教，不是我的尊嚴。
硬幣會滾走，但我願不願意好好對人，從來都是自己決定的。

en.content:
Coins tossed on the counter roll.
When I bend down to pick one up,
it's not the coin I'm retrieving —
it's the reminder: the pay can be low, but the spine stays straight.
en.editorNote: For the worker whose customer flung money onto the counter. You pick up the coin; what you can't pick up is the basic respect they should have shown.
variations: When a customer throws money on the counter, what they're throwing away is their own manners — not my dignity.
The coin rolls away, but whether I treat people decently has always been my call, not theirs.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the 「客人能客訴員工，員工卻不能客訴奧客」 power-imbalance observation (Dcard 心情) into a wry imagined-feature joke

zh-tw.content:
客人可以填一張客訴單把我寫進去，
我多希望也有一張表，
標題寫「客戶服務評分：本次客人，建議下架」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又收到一張客訴單的同事。客訴是單向的，這就是為什麼好脾氣的人特別容易被磨光。
variations: 如果奧客也能被退貨，我們的庫存應該每天都會少很多。
客人對我們的評分是五星制，我們對客人的評分是『只能默默記在心裡』制。

en.content:
A customer can file a complaint form with my name on it.
I just wish there were a form for us too,
titled: "Customer rating: this one — recommend discontinuing."
en.editorNote: For the coworker who got handed another complaint form today. Complaints only run one direction — which is exactly why the patient ones get worn down fastest.
variations: If difficult customers could be returned for a refund, our inventory would shrink a lot every single day.
Customers rate us on a five-star scale; we rate customers on a "keep it quietly to yourself" scale.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the canonical 「我之前都可以，為什麼現在不行」 line from a Dcard 心情 list of 奧客經典語錄 into a fresh exchange

zh-tw.content:
「我之前來都可以這樣，為什麼現在不行？」
「因為上次幫您的那位同事，已經被您弄走了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又被「我之前都可以」開頭逼到牆角的同事。所謂的『之前可以』，常常是某個人破例幫忙、然後默默離職的結果。
variations: 客人說的「之前都可以」，翻譯成白話就是「之前有人不敢拒絕我」。
規定沒變，變的是這次站在你面前的不是會破例的那位。

en.content:
"I've always been able to do it this way before — why not now?"
"Because the coworker who used to make exceptions for you finally quit."
en.editorNote: For anyone backed into a corner by the "but I've always been able to" opener today. "Always been able to" usually means someone once bent the rule for you and then quietly resigned.
variations: "I've always been able to before" translates to "someone before was too scared to say no."
The policy didn't change. What changed is that the person in front of you this time isn't the one who breaks the rules.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the 「別家店都可以，只有你們不行」 comparison trope from the Dcard 奧客經典語錄 thread

zh-tw.content:
「別家都可以這樣，只有你們不行。」
「那真是辛苦您，特地跑來我們這家不行的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被『別家都可以』比較到無語的同事。如果別家真的什麼都可以，那位客人應該早就在別家了，不會站在你面前。
variations: 「別家都可以」這句話最神奇的地方是：那麼好的別家，客人卻偏偏選擇來罵你這家。
別家可不可以我不知道，我只知道別家現在很慶幸這位客人在你這。

en.content:
"Every other place lets me — only you guys won't."
"Then thank you for going out of your way to come to the one place that won't."
en.editorNote: For the coworker worn down by the "every other place allows it" comparison today. If those other places really allowed everything, that customer would already be there — not standing in front of you.
variations: The magic of "every other place lets me" is that, given all those wonderful other places, the customer still chose to come yell at yours.
I don't know whether the other places allow it. I just know the other places are very glad this customer is at yours right now.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the Dcard 調查局 segment on 服務業 being 「月薪3萬的全能人力」 expected to do everything; rewritten as a job-description joke

zh-tw.content:
職缺寫：「歡迎新鮮人，工作輕鬆。」
上工第一天才發現：
收銀、補貨、客訴、打掃、搬重物、被罵——
薪水是一份，職務是六份。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每個被『工作輕鬆』四個字騙進來的人。服務業的薪水算的是一個人，工作量算的是一個團隊。
variations: 他們說的「一人多工」，意思是『一份薪水，請你扮演整間店』。
面試時的「工作內容單純」，跟實際上的「什麼都你來」，中間隔著一整本員工手冊。

en.content:
The job post said: "Newcomers welcome, easy work."
Day one I found out:
register, restocking, complaints, cleaning, heavy lifting, getting yelled at —
one salary, six job descriptions.
en.editorNote: For everyone lured in by the words "easy work." In service, the pay is calculated for one person; the workload is calculated for a whole team.
variations: Their "multitasking role" means "one paycheck to play the entire store by yourself."
Between the interview's "simple duties" and the reality of "you handle everything" sits an entire employee handbook.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the 「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題」 deflection line from the Dcard 奧客經典語錄 thread into a warmer 心聲 piece

zh-tw.content:
客人說：「那是你們的問題，不是我的問題。」
我笑著點頭，
心裡只記得一件事——
他的問題會散場，我的下班會準時到。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被推了一身『那是你們的問題』的服務業朋友。把問題全丟給你的人，通常自己一個問題都不想扛；別讓他的情緒住進你的下班時間。
variations: 他可以說那是我的問題，但他沒辦法決定我幾點放下它。
客人的問題是一時的，我的人生是一輩子的，這筆帳我算得很清楚。

en.content:
The customer says, "That's your problem, not mine."
I smile and nod,
holding on to just one thing —
his problem will clear out, and my shift will end on time.
en.editorNote: For the worker buried under "that's your problem" today. People who dump every problem on you usually won't carry a single one themselves — don't let their mood move into your off-hours.
variations: He can call it my problem, but he doesn't get to decide what time I set it down.
The customer's problem is temporary; my life is permanent. I keep that math very clear.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the canonical 「別家店都可以，只有你們店不行」 deflection from the 2026-04 Dcard 奧客經典語錄 thread into a punchy two-line exchange

zh-tw.content:
「別家店都可以，為什麼你們不行？」
「那您要不要去那家可以的店？」
「我就是不想去啊。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又被『別家店都可以』綁架的同事。真相是：那家神奇的『別家店』，通常跟『我朋友』住在同一個查無此地的地址。
variations: 客人口中的「別家店」，是這個世界上規矩最寬、距離最遠、永遠不去的那家店。
「別家店都可以」翻譯成白話：我知道你不行，我只是想看你為難。

en.content:
"Every other store allows it. Why can't you?"
"Would you like to go to that other store, then?"
"No, I don't want to."
en.editorNote: For the coworker held hostage by "every other store does it" again today. The truth: that magical "other store" lives at the same address-not-found as "my friend."
variations: The customer's "other store" is the one place on earth with the loosest rules, the farthest away, that they'll never actually visit.
"Every other store allows it" translates to: I know you can't, I just want to watch you squirm.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the TwistedSifter piece on the 'retail laugh' and the millionth 'that must mean it's free' joke at the scanner; rewritten as a Taiwanese 心聲 about the reflex smile

zh-tw.content:
東西刷不過，
客人說：「沒掃到代表免費吧？」
我笑了，
那是今天第十八次，
同一個笑話，同一個笑。
我的笑早就不是反應，是設定。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天要對同一個冷笑話笑十遍的收銀同事。那不是你假，是你的臉學會了在心放假的時候繼續上班。
variations: 服務業的笑，分兩種：真的笑，跟『第十八次了我還是得笑』的笑。
客人以為他很幽默，其實他只是排在前面那十七個人後面。

en.content:
The item won't scan.
The customer says, "Guess that means it's free?"
I laugh —
the eighteenth time today,
same joke, same laugh.
My laugh stopped being a reaction a long time ago. It's a setting.
en.editorNote: For the cashier who laughs at the same tired joke ten times a shift. It's not that you're fake — it's that your face learned to keep working even when your heart clocked out.
variations: There are two kinds of service-industry laughs: the real one, and the "that's the eighteenth time but I still have to" one.
The customer thinks he's hilarious. He's really just standing behind the seventeen who said it first.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the 「叫你們店長/經理出來」 escalation trope from the 2026-04 Dcard 奧客經典語錄 thread

zh-tw.content:
「叫你們店長出來！」
「我就是店長。」
「那叫你們老闆出來！」
「我就是老闆。」
「……那叫個能做主的出來。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被『叫主管出來』連環追殺的同事。有些客人要的不是答案，是一個比你大的人能讓他繼續鬧。
variations: 客人要找的「能做主的人」，其實是「能讓步的人」，這兩個在他字典裡是同義詞。
升到店長那天我才懂，原來『叫主管出來』之後，下一個被叫的人就是我自己。

en.content:
"Get your manager!"
"I am the manager."
"Then get your boss!"
"I am the boss."
"...then get someone who can actually make decisions."
en.editorNote: For the coworker hunted down the chain of command today. Some customers don't want an answer — they want someone bigger than you so they can keep going.
variations: The "someone who can make decisions" the customer wants is really "someone who will cave." In their dictionary those are synonyms.
The day I got promoted to manager, I finally understood: after "get me your manager," the next person they call out is me.

items.sourceNote: Reworks the 「我之前都可以，為什麼現在不行」 line from the 2026-04 Dcard 奧客經典語錄 thread into a warmer 心聲 piece about boundaries

zh-tw.content:
「我之前都可以，為什麼現在不行？」
以前可以，是因為有人替你破例，
然後那個人被罵。
今天換我守規矩，
不是我變了，是我不想成為下一個被罵的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被『我之前都可以』情勒的服務業朋友。別人為了息事寧人破的例，從來不該變成你必須背的債。守規矩不是刁難，是不讓上一個受傷的人白白受傷。
variations: 「之前可以」這三個字背後，常常站著一個替客人破例、然後挨了罵的前同事。
我守的不是規定，是那條讓我下班還能抬頭做人的線。

en.content:
"You let me before. Why not now?"
Before, someone broke the rule for you —
and then that someone got reamed out.
Today I'm holding the line.
I didn't change. I just don't want to be the next one who gets reamed out.
en.editorNote: For the worker guilt-tripped with "you let me before" today. A rule someone bent to keep the peace should never become a debt you have to carry. Holding the line isn't being difficult — it's making sure the last person who got hurt didn't get hurt for nothing.
variations: Behind "you let me before" there's usually a former coworker who bent the rule for the customer and got chewed out for it.
I'm not defending a policy. I'm defending the line that lets me still hold my head up after clocking out.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the 'Working hard or hardly working?' recurring joke every support/retail worker hears; rewritten with a Taiwan-specific 上班摸魚 counter-angle

zh-tw.content:
客人：「認真上班還是在打混啊？」
我笑著說：「打混。」
他愣住了，
因為他準備了三秒的哈哈大笑，用不上了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天又被客人用「你們是不是很閒」開玩笑的服務業朋友。這種玩笑你聽第一千次了，配一句「下次就這樣回」。
variations: 客人問「很閒喔」，其實他只是想確認自己等一下可以插隊。
他不是關心你累不累，他是想確認等下有沒有人理他。

en.content:
Customer: "Working hard, or hardly working?"
I smile: "Hardly working."
He freezes —
because the big laugh he'd loaded up has nowhere to go now.
en.editorNote: For the worker who heard "working hard or hardly working?" for the thousandth time today. Send it with "try this next time." The line stopped being funny on day one.
variations: When they ask "slow day, huh?" they're really just checking whether they can cut the line.
He's not asking if you're tired. He's asking if someone will help him faster.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the classic 'I'll take my business elsewhere' threat trope in customer-service memes; rewritten as a two-line exchange

zh-tw.content:
「我以後不會再來了！」
「好的，路上小心。」
「你這什麼態度！」
先生，您剛剛才是威脅我的那位。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給被客人用「我再也不來了」勒索過的同事。這句話的威力，通常跟他消費的金額成反比。
variations: 「我不會再來了」是奧客版的「我數到三」，數完他還是站在原地。
威脅要走的客人，往往是最捨不得走的那一個。

en.content:
"I'm never coming back here again!"
"Understood. Take care getting home."
"What kind of attitude is that?!"
Sir, you're the one who just threatened to leave.
en.editorNote: For the coworker held hostage by "I'm never coming back." The power of that threat is usually inversely proportional to how much they actually spend.
variations: "I'm never coming back" is the customer version of "I'm counting to three" — and they're still standing there after three.
The customer who threatens to walk is usually the one least willing to.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the 'closing time customer' recurring service-industry pain point; original scenario built around a Taiwan 打烊 setting

zh-tw.content:
打烊前五分鐘，門推開。
「還沒關吧？」
沒關，因為就差你這一位還沒進來。
燈是為你亮的，也是為你關不掉的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天都在打烊前被最後一組客人「加時賽」的餐飲同事。你不是討厭客人，你只是想準時看到自己家的燈。
variations: 打烊前推門的那一位，通常也是點最多、走最慢的那一位。
「還沒關吧」——這四個字讓下班時間變成一個傳說。

en.content:
Five minutes to close, the door swings open.
"You're not closed yet, right?"
No — you were the only one we were waiting on.
The lights are on for you. They also won't turn off because of you.
en.editorNote: For the food-service worker who plays overtime every night thanks to the last table in. You don't hate the customer — you'd just like to see your own front door before midnight.
variations: The one who pushes the door at closing time usually orders the most and leaves the slowest.
"You're not closed yet, right?" — four words that turn clocking out into a legend.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by the 'call center runs on passive aggression and deep breathing' pun; rewritten as an internal-monologue call-center piece

zh-tw.content:
「您好，很高興為您服務。」
這句話我今天說了兩百次，
開心指數一次比一次低，
但語氣一次比一次穩——
這就是專業。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天要把同一句歡迎詞說到失去意義的客服同事。聲音的穩定，是用心裡的崩潰換來的。
variations: 客服的專業，是把「我快瘋了」講成「很高興為您服務」。
電話那頭的笑聲是免費的，但那笑聲的成本，只有戴耳機的人知道。

en.content:
"Hello, thank you for calling — how can I help?"
I've said it two hundred times today.
My joy drops with each one,
but my tone gets steadier with each one —
that's what they call professional.
en.editorNote: For the call-center agent who has to say the same greeting until it loses all meaning. A steady voice is bought with the meltdown you're hiding behind it.
variations: Call-center professionalism is saying "I'm losing it" as "how can I help you today?"
The cheerful voice on the line is free. Only the person in the headset knows what it cost.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 心情 board 奧客語錄 thread; rewritten around the condescending 'you must be new here' put-down

zh-tw.content:
「你新來的喔？這都不會。」
我在這五年了。
只是規定沒有為您改，
您就當作我還沒學會。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被客人用「你新來的喔」瞧不起的同事。你不是不會，你是不肯為一個人破壞所有人的規矩。
variations: 「你新來的喔」翻譯過來就是「你怎麼敢不聽我的」。
資歷五年，還是輸給一句「別家都可以」——但輸的是規定，不是你。

en.content:
"You must be new here — you don't even know this?"
I've been here five years.
The rule just didn't bend for you,
so you decided I hadn't learned it yet.
en.editorNote: For the worker talked down to with "you must be new" today. You're not incompetent — you just won't break everyone's rules for one person.
variations: "You must be new here" translates to "how dare you not do what I say."
Five years in and still losing to "other places allow it" — but it's the rule that lost, not you.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 心情 board 奧客語錄 thread listing classic 'that's your problem, not mine' put-downs; rewritten around the customer weaponizing 'the customer is always right'

zh-tw.content:
「顧客永遠是對的。」
是啊，
所以我永遠是錯的，
連我沒說的話都是。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天被「顧客永遠是對的」這句老話壓在地上摩擦的人。這句話原本是叫店家用心，不是叫客人免責。
variations: 「顧客永遠是對的」下一句其實是「所以請對得起這句話」，只是很多人只背前半段。
如果顧客永遠是對的，那我這張道歉臉是演給誰看的。

en.content:
"The customer is always right."
Sure —
which makes me always wrong,
even about things I never said.
en.editorNote: For anyone dragged across the floor by that old line today. It was meant to tell shops to care, not to hand customers a get-out-of-jail card.
variations: "The customer is always right" has a second half — "so act like it" — that a lot of people conveniently forget.
If the customer is always right, who exactly is this apology face for?

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 2026 customer-service burnout reporting noting agents now absorb a higher concentration of emotionally charged calls as self-service handles the easy ones; rewritten as a first-person voice quote

zh-tw.content:
機器人負責回答簡單的問題，
我負責承接崩潰的人。
升級的是系統，
耗損的是我。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給被自動化留下最難那一塊的客服。你面對的不是問題，是已經被機器人惹到爆炸才轉給你的人。
variations: 自助結帳搶走了輕鬆的單，把「我要投訴」全部留給了人類。
科技進步的意思是：簡單的都不用人做了，只剩最傷的那些是我的。

en.content:
The bot handles the easy questions.
I handle the people falling apart.
The system got the upgrade;
I got the wear and tear.
en.editorNote: For the agent automation left with the hardest slice. You're not facing a problem — you're facing someone the bot already pushed over the edge before transferring them to you.
variations: Self-checkout took all the easy orders and left every "I want to complain" for the humans.
Progress means the simple stuff no longer needs a person — only the parts that hurt are still mine.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by Dcard 奧客語錄 thread featuring 'I was able to do this before, why not now'; rewritten around a customer treating a one-time exception as a permanent right

zh-tw.content:
「上次你們可以，這次為什麼不行？」
上次是我通融，
您把好心當成菜單，
以為每一次都能點。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給今天因為「上次好心幫忙」被當成永久福利的同事。通融一次是人情，不是簽約，別讓善意變成把柄。
variations: 「上次可以」的意思是「有人幫你破例」，不是「規定改了」。
把別人的通融記成自己的權利，是奧客最擅長的記帳方式。

en.content:
"You let me last time — why not now?"
Last time I bent the rule for you.
You filed a kindness as a menu item
and assumed you could order it every visit.
en.editorNote: For the coworker whose one-time favor got logged as a permanent perk today. Bending a rule once is a courtesy, not a contract — don't let goodwill become leverage.
variations: "You did it last time" means "someone made an exception," not "the rule changed."
Booking someone else's kindness as your own entitlement is a difficult customer's favorite accounting trick.

items.sourceNote: Inspired by 2026 burnout reporting on the demand to always be 'on'; rewritten around the gap between the smile at work and the silence at home

zh-tw.content:
上班笑一整天，
回家一句話都不想說。
不是你變冷淡了，
是你的溫柔都被今天的客人領走了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給下班後只想安靜的服務業家人朋友。他們不是不愛你，是那份笑容已經被上班用完，需要時間重新長回來。
variations: 服務業的沉默不是生氣，是把整天的笑臉還完之後，剩下的那一點自己。
白天發完了全世界的耐心，晚上就沒力氣對最愛的人多說一句了。

en.content:
Smiling all day at work,
then not a word left at home.
You didn't turn cold —
your warmth just got claimed by today's customers.
en.editorNote: For the service-industry loved one who only wants quiet after a shift. It's not that they stopped caring — that smile got spent on the job and needs time to grow back.
variations: A service worker's silence isn't anger. It's the little bit of self that's left after handing out smiles all day.
Give the whole world your patience by daylight and there's none left for the people you love by night.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content: 非常抱歉讓您久等了，這次的狀況確實是我們這邊的疏失。退款我已經幫您立即處理，款項會在三到五個工作天內退回原付款帳戶。造成您的不便，真的很不好意思。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：處理已確認為公司疏失的退款客訴，先道歉再給明確時程
variations: 這部分是我們沒做好，退款我馬上為您送出，三到五個工作天會入帳，還請您稍等一下。
真的很抱歉造成您的困擾，退款已經在處理中，我會全程幫您盯著進度。

en.content: I'm really sorry for the wait, and this one is on us. I've gone ahead and processed your refund right away. It should land back on your original payment method within three to five business days. I truly apologize for the hassle.
en.editorNote: For refund complaints confirmed as company error: apologize, then give a clear timeline
variations: That's on us, and I've already pushed your refund through. It'll show up in three to five business days. Sorry for the trouble.
I'm so sorry about this. Your refund is being processed now, and I'll keep an eye on it until it's done.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content: 您想要的這款目前暫時缺貨，預計下週會補到貨。如果方便的話，我先幫您登記，一到貨就馬上通知您，這樣就不用擔心錯過囉。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：商品缺貨時主動提供到貨通知服務，降低顧客流失
variations: 這款剛好賣完了，下週會再進貨，我幫您留個聯絡方式，補到貨第一時間通知您好嗎？
目前沒有現貨，不過很快就會補上，要不要我先幫您預留一組？到貨立刻聯絡您。

en.content: The one you're after is out of stock right now, but we're expecting more next week. If you'd like, I can put you on the list and give you a heads-up the moment it's back, so you don't miss it.
en.editorNote: When an item is out of stock: proactively offer a back-in-stock notification to keep the customer
variations: We just sold out of that one, but a new batch is coming next week. Want me to grab your info and ping you as soon as it lands?
No stock at the moment, but it won't be long. I can set one aside for you and reach out the second it arrives.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content: 這個部分依照我們目前的規定沒辦法直接處理，真的很抱歉。不過我幫您想了另一個做法，應該可以達到差不多的效果，我說明給您聽看看合不合適？
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：無法答應顧客原始要求時，改以替代方案軟性婉拒
variations: 很抱歉這樣做我們這邊沒辦法，但我有另一個建議，或許更符合您的需求，讓我跟您分享一下。
這項目前規定上真的不行，不好意思，我幫您找了個替代方案，聽聽看有沒有幫助？

en.content: I'm sorry, but under our current policy I'm not able to do that directly. That said, I've thought of another approach that should get you pretty much the same result. Can I walk you through it and see if it works for you?
en.editorNote: When you can't grant the original request: soften the no with an alternative
variations: Unfortunately that's not something we can do on our end, but I've got another idea that might fit even better. Mind if I share it?
I really can't do that one, sorry, but here's a workaround I found. Let me know if it helps.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 「叫你們店長出來」奧客經典行為，改寫成內心 OS 反轉哏

zh-tw.content:
客人：「叫你們店長出來！」
我：「好的，我就是店長。」
客人：「⋯⋯那叫你們老闆出來。」
原來所謂的「找上面的人」，
真正的意思是
「找一個會答應我的人」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：傳給每天被要求「叫上面出來」的門市主管。重點是奧客要的從來不是層級，是妥協。
variations: 「我要投訴，叫主管來。」主管來了，是我。他的臉瞬間垮下來——因為他要的不是主管，是靠山。
客人堅持要找「有權限的人」，找到最後發現，最有權限說不的那個人，一直站在他面前。

en.content:
Customer: "Get me your manager!"
Me: "Sure — I'm the manager."
Customer: "...then get me the owner."
Turns out "talk to someone higher up"
never meant higher up.
It meant
"find someone who'll say yes."
en.editorNote: For any shift lead who gets 'let me speak to your manager' daily. The demand was never about rank — it was about finding a yes.
variations: "I want to file a complaint. Get your supervisor." The supervisor showed up. It was me. His face dropped — he didn't want a supervisor, he wanted an ally.
He insisted on someone "with the authority." Turns out the person with the most authority to say no was standing in front of him the whole time.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 「掃不出來就是免費」retail 經典哏，改寫成台灣賣場語氣

zh-tw.content:
商品條碼掃不出來，
客人眼睛一亮：「那是不是免費的？」
第一次聽覺得好笑，
第一百次聽——
我開始相信，
這句話是全國同步發布的公告，
只是沒人通知店員。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：傳給收銀台的戰友。這句話的殺傷力不在內容，在於每個人都以為自己是第一個想到的。
variations: 掃不出來的當下，總有人要補一句「那免費囉？」我都想回：先生，這台機器壞的是感應器，不是店規。
條碼一嗶不過，客人的反應永遠只有兩種：嘆氣，或者眼睛發亮說「免費喔」。後者，我一天要拒絕八次。

en.content:
The barcode won't scan,
and the customer lights up: "Guess it's free, then?"
The first time, it was funny.
By the hundredth time,
I'm convinced
this line went out as a nationwide memo —
nobody just forgot to tell the cashiers.
en.editorNote: For the folks behind the register. The joke doesn't hurt because of what it says — it hurts because everyone thinks they invented it.
variations: Every time the scanner chokes, someone has to add "so it's free?" Sir, the broken part is the sensor, not the price policy.
When the barcode won't beep, there are only two reactions: a sigh, or the eyes lighting up with "free, right?" I turn down the second one eight times a day.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 「別家都可以只有你們不行」奧客語錄，改寫成金句

zh-tw.content:
「別家店都可以，只有你們不行。」
那您真的很推薦別家，
連他們的規定都幫他們記得這麼清楚。
只是我很好奇：
如果別家真的什麼都可以，
您今天為什麼還站在我面前？
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：傳給被「別家都可以」情緒勒索過的每一位。這句話的破綻，往往就在說話的人自己身上。
variations: 客人說「別人家都通融」，我心裡想：那家店真幸運，被您這樣天天惦記著，卻剛好今天沒開。
「你們是我遇過最不方便的一家。」謝謝您把這句話，用同樣的語氣，說給每一家聽。

en.editorNote: For anyone guilt-tripped with 'everyone else allows it.' The hole in that line usually sits with the person saying it.
variations: The customer says "everyone else makes an exception." Lucky them — remembered so fondly, yet somehow closed today.
"You're the most inconvenient place I've ever been to." Thanks — I'm sure you deliver that same line, same tone, to every place you've ever been.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 情緒勞動/微笑面具主題，延續 svc-021、svc-081 的心聲風格

zh-tw.content:
下班脫下制服的那一刻，
我以為我卸下了工作。
其實真正卸不下的，
是那張掛了一整天的笑臉。
它太久沒放下，
連要恢復成自己的表情，
都得練習一下。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給每個回到家還笑不出來、也哭不太出來的服務業朋友。情緒勞動最重的部分，是下班後還在結帳。
variations: 制服可以掛回衣櫃，可是那個微笑，常常掛在臉上忘了拿下來，一路帶回家。
有人問我下班怎麼都面無表情。因為我一整天的表情，早就在櫃檯前用完了。

en.content:
The moment I take off the uniform after a shift,
I think I've left the job behind.
But the thing I can't take off
is the smile I wore all day.
I've held it up so long
that getting my own face back
takes a little practice.
en.editorNote: For every service worker who gets home and can't quite smile — or cry. The heaviest part of emotional labor is the bill that comes due after clocking out.
variations: The uniform goes back in the closet, but the smile? It stays stuck on and rides all the way home with me.
Someone asked why I look blank after work. Because I spent every last expression I had at the counter today.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 「我要找你們經理」升級投訴橋段，改寫成金句；延續奧客語錄風格

zh-tw.content:
「叫你們經理出來！」
經理來了，說的話跟我一模一樣。
您沉默三秒，
然後說：「那再叫更上面的。」
先生，再往上，就只剩天了，
而祂通常不接客訴。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：傳給今天被「叫你們主管出來」連環攻擊的第一線同事。奧客要的從來不是答案，是一個比你更容易被他壓過的人。
variations: 客人說要找經理，其實只是想換一個人聽同一個規定，然後假裝那個規定變了。
「你做不了主。」對，我做不了主，但奇怪的是，能做主的那位，講的跟我一字不差。

en.content:
"Get me your manager!"
The manager comes over and says exactly what I said.
You go quiet for three seconds,
then: "Then get me whoever's above them."
Sir, above them there's only the sky —
and it doesn't usually take complaints.
en.editorNote: For anyone hit today with the endless 'get me your supervisor.' What they want isn't an answer — it's someone they think will fold easier than you.
variations: Asking for the manager is really just asking to hear the same policy from a different mouth, then pretending it changed.
"You can't make that call." True — but funny how the person who can says the exact same words I just did.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 「你怎麼不笑」被要求微笑的橋段（2026-04 viral），改寫成情緒勞動心聲

zh-tw.content:
「你上班怎麼都不笑一個？」
因為我的笑，是有配額的。
那一份，我留給了
三分鐘前對我大吼的那位。
您來的時候，剛好用完了，
下一批要等我把心情補貨。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給被要求「笑一個」的服務業朋友。微笑是我們的工具，不是你的權利；用完了，就是用完了。
variations: 客人嫌我臭臉，可是他不知道，我這張臉今天已經對五十個人笑過了，剩下的額度，真的擠不出來。
「服務業就是要笑口常開。」是啊，只是沒人告訴客人，那個笑，是我們自己一格一格充上去的。

en.content:
"Why don't you ever smile at work?"
Because my smiles come with a daily quota.
That one, I already gave
to the guy who screamed at me three minutes ago.
You showed up right as it ran out —
the next batch is still restocking.
en.editorNote: For anyone told to 'give us a smile.' The smile is our tool, not your entitlement. When it's spent, it's spent.
variations: A customer called me sour-faced. He didn't know this face already smiled at fifty people today — there's just none left to squeeze out.
"Service work means smiling all day." Sure — nobody tells the customer that smile gets charged back up one bar at a time, by us.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 客人把店員當「情緒支援專員」倒垃圾（2026-05 viral），改寫成心聲金句

zh-tw.content:
我只是問了一句「今天還好嗎」，
您就把整個禮拜的委屈，
連同對前老闆、對前任、對這個世界的怒火，
全部倒進我這個小小的櫃檯。
我的職稱是店員，
不是您沒付錢的情緒回收站。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：傳給那個下班回到家、還在幫全世界收情緒的客服或櫃檯朋友。禮貌問候不等於免費諮商。
variations: 客人把我當樹洞，問題是樹洞不用上班，也沒有業績壓力，還可以裝作沒聽到。
我這個位置，聽過的心事比里長還多，領的卻是最低時薪。

en.content:
I just asked, "How's your day going?"
and you poured a whole week's worth of hurt into me —
the rage at your last boss, your ex, the entire world —
all dumped onto my little counter.
My title is clerk,
not the unpaid recycling bin for your feelings.
en.editorNote: For the counter or call-center friend who gets home still carrying everyone else's emotions. A polite hello is not free therapy.
variations: Customers treat me like a confession booth — except the booth doesn't clock in, doesn't have sales targets, and can pretend it didn't hear.
This seat has heard more secrets than a priest, and gets paid minimum wage to do it.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 2026 餐飲業缺工潮（每4家1家缺人），改寫成缺工現場心聲

zh-tw.content:
客人問：「怎麼這麼慢，你們人呢？」
我想說：人就是我。
外場我、內場我、收銀我，
連剛剛幫您帶位的，也是我。
我不是動作慢，
我是同時在當三個人，還要記得微笑。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給缺工現場一打三、一打五的每一位。你不是慢，你是把整間店扛在肩上。
variations: 「你們可以多派幾個人嗎？」可以啊，等你先告訴我上哪找。
以前一個蘿蔔一個坑，現在是一個蘿蔔補三個坑，還要笑著跟你說歡迎光臨。

en.content:
The customer asks, "Why so slow? Where's your staff?"
I want to say: the staff is me.
Front of house — me. Kitchen — me. Register — me.
The one who seated you just now? Also me.
I'm not slow —
I'm being three people at once, and still remembering to smile.
en.editorNote: For everyone running a whole floor solo during the staffing crunch. You're not slow — you're carrying the entire place on your shoulders.
variations: "Can't you put more people on?" Sure — right after you tell me where to find them.
It used to be one person per station. Now it's one person covering three, still cheerfully saying 'welcome in.'

items.sourceNote: internal only — 2026-05 Dcard 調查局「台灣服務業有多累」影片，第一線人員怕被客訴、被公司河蟹只能隱忍的心聲，改寫成台前微笑台後吞聲的對照

zh-tw.content:
您說要客訴我，
我點點頭，說「好的，這是意見卡」。
您不知道的是——
在您寫下第一個字之前，
公司已經先寫好我的道歉，
連我有沒有錯都不用問。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給那些一被威脅客訴，公司連問都不問就先要你道歉的第一線同事。你不是怕客人，你是怕沒人站在你這邊。
variations: 「我要投訴」四個字，在我們公司的意思是「你先道歉，理由之後再說」。
客人手上那張意見卡，比我三年的年資還有份量。

en.content:
You said you'd file a complaint about me.
I nodded and said, "Of course — here's a feedback card."
What you don't know:
before you write the first word,
the company already drafted my apology —
no one even asked whether I did anything wrong.
en.editorNote: For the frontline coworker whose company makes them apologize before anyone checks the facts. You're not scared of customers — you're scared no one's on your side.
variations: "I want to complain" translates, in my company, to "you apologize first, we'll find a reason later."
That feedback card in the customer's hand carries more weight than my three years of tenure.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 2026-05 顧客把酸黃瓜丟到店員臉上要求重做、拿到後又問酸黃瓜在哪的荒謬案例，改寫成餐飲現場的無限迴圈

zh-tw.content:
「這個我不要，重做。」
（重做）
「欸，我要的那個呢？」
先生，您剛剛親手把它丟到我臉上了，
它現在跟您的耐心一樣，
在地上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給每天被「重做」跟「你們怎麼做的」夾殺的餐飲人。你沒做錯，只是站在客人記性最差的那一天。
variations: 客人要的東西，通常在他自己剛剛丟掉的那個袋子裡。
重做三次還是同一份，因為問題從來不在那份餐點。

en.content:
"I don't want this. Remake it."
(remade)
"Hey, where's the thing I wanted?"
Sir, you threw it at my face two minutes ago.
It's on the floor now,
right next to your patience.
en.editorNote: For the food-service crew stuck in the loop of "remake it" and "how'd you mess this up." You didn't do anything wrong — you just caught the customer on the day their memory took off.
variations: The thing the customer's asking for is usually in the bag they just threw out.
Third remake, same order — because the problem was never the food.

items.sourceNote: internal only — Not Always Right 零售員工「先服務排隊守規矩的人，把插隊奧客留到最後」的經典還擊，改寫成排隊正義

zh-tw.content:
您大聲說「我很急，先幫我」，
於是我很有效率地，
先幫了您前面那七位，
每一位都幫得特別仔細。
輪到您的時候我笑著說：
「久等了，急的人我都服務完了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給那些不能翻臉、只能用流程反擊的第一線英雄。守規矩的客人，值得你把最好的耐心留給他們。
variations: 插隊的人最怕的不是拒絕，是我照順序、慢慢來、完全合規。
我沒有偏心，我只是對排隊的人特別溫柔。

en.content:
You announced, "I'm in a hurry, help me first."
So I got very efficient —
I helped the seven people ahead of you,
each one with extra care.
When it was finally your turn I smiled:
"Thanks for waiting. I've served everyone who was in a rush."
en.editorNote: For the frontline hero who can't snap back, only fight with process. Customers who wait their turn deserve your very best patience.
variations: The line-cutter's real fear isn't being refused — it's me going in order, slowly, completely by the book.
I'm not playing favorites. I'm just extra gentle with the people who waited.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 「顧客永遠是對的」被濫用來合理化奧客行為的討論，改寫成拆穿這句話的心聲

zh-tw.content:
「顧客永遠是對的。」
這句話原本的意思是，
客人喜歡什麼，我們就多賣什麼。
不是——
客人想羞辱誰，我們就得站著讓他羞辱誰。
這中間差了一整個人的尊嚴。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給每個被這句話壓著吞下委屈的人。這句話從來不包含「員工要無條件被欺負」，是有人故意讀錯。
variations: 「顧客永遠是對的」下一句，其實是「在他選要買什麼的時候」，不是在他選要罵誰的時候。
把一句行銷格言，硬凹成欺負人的免罪金牌，這才是真正的斷章取義。

en.content:
"The customer is always right."
The phrase originally meant:
sell more of whatever customers want to buy.
It did NOT mean:
whoever a customer wants to humiliate, we stand there and take it.
The difference between those two is one whole person's dignity.
en.editorNote: For everyone who's had this line used to make them swallow abuse. It never meant "staff must be mistreated on demand" — someone's just reading it wrong on purpose.
variations: "The customer is always right" ends with "about what they want to buy" — not about who they get to yell at.
Twisting a marketing slogan into a permission slip for cruelty — that's the real quote taken out of context.

items.sourceNote: internal only — Bored Panda 客服員工回顧「至今仍難忘」的離奇互動，改寫成下班了腦中還在重播的職業病

zh-tw.content:
下班了，制服脫了，
可是那句「你們這什麼態度」，
還在我腦裡循環播放。
奇怪的是，我明明笑到臉都僵了，
他卻聽成了態度。
原來我最需要客訴的人，是我自己——
客訴我把別人的情緒帶回了家。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給那個下班很久、還在腦中重播白天那句話的服務業朋友。把制服掛回衣櫃時，記得把那句話也一起留在那裡。
variations: 職業病就是：客人早就忘了那句話，我卻幫他記了一整晚。
我微笑的肌肉記得所有客人，客人卻連我長怎樣都不記得。

en.content:
Off the clock, uniform off,
but that line — "what kind of attitude is this" —
is still on loop in my head.
The weird part: I smiled till my face went numb,
and he still heard an attitude.
Turns out the person I most need to complain about is me —
for carrying someone else's mood home.
en.editorNote: For the service friend who clocked out hours ago and is still replaying today's line. When you hang the uniform back up, try to leave that sentence hanging there too.
variations: Occupational hazard: the customer forgot that line instantly, and I remembered it for them all night.
My smile muscles remember every customer. The customers don't even remember my face.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 「隔壁店可以，你們為什麼不行」是 Dcard 奧客語錄的經典開場，改寫成拆穿這句話邏輯的心聲

zh-tw.content:
「隔壁那家可以，你們為什麼不行？」
那請問，
您現在是站在隔壁那家，
還是站在我這家？
如果隔壁真的什麼都比較好，
那您走進來的這扇門，
應該是走錯了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給每天被拿去跟「隔壁那家」比較的第一線人員。奇怪的是，那家什麼都好，客人卻永遠站在你面前。
variations: 客人口中的「隔壁那家」，永遠比較便宜、比較快、比較好——好到他還是選擇來罵你這家。
「別家都可以」如果是真的，那您真的可以去別家，我是認真的。

en.content:
"The place next door can do it — why can't you?"
Quick question:
are you standing next door right now,
or are you standing in front of me?
Because if next door really does everything better,
then the door you just walked through
was the wrong one.
en.editorNote: For the front-liner who gets compared to "the place next door" every single day. Funny how that place is better at everything, yet the customer keeps choosing to stand here yelling at you.
variations: The customer's "other store" is always cheaper, faster, and nicer — so nice they still came here to complain instead.
"Everywhere else lets me" — great, then everywhere else is genuinely welcome to have you. I mean that.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 零售員工最受不了的「掃不到就免費啦」客人自以為幽默的老哏，改寫成聽第八百遍的心聲

zh-tw.content:
商品掃不到，
客人笑著說：「那就免費送我啦！」
先生，這句話
今天您是第七位講的，
上一位在上午十點半，
他也覺得只有他想到。
我的笑是真的，
笑點不是。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給每個被「掃不到就免費」「你們生意這麼好一定賺翻」這種自以為新哏轟炸的收銀朋友。你的笑肌很敬業，你的耳朵很想罷工。
variations: 「掃不到免費」這句哏的專利，早在二十年前就過期了，但客人每天都以為是自己剛發明的。
客人以為講了全場最好笑的話，其實他是今天的第七個接力棒。

en.content:
The item won't scan,
and the customer grins: "Guess it's free then!"
Sir, that line —
you're the seventh person to say it today.
The last one was at ten thirty a.m.,
and he also thought he was the only one clever enough.
My smile is real.
The joke isn't.
en.editorNote: For every cashier bombed with "it won't scan, so it's free!" and "business is booming, you must be rich!" Your smile muscles are consummate professionals. Your ears want to file for leave.
variations: The patent on the "guess it's free" joke expired twenty years ago — customers just reinvent it fresh every single morning.
The customer thinks he just landed the funniest line of the day. He's actually the seventh runner in the relay.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 「叫你們經理出來」的 Karen 老哏，改寫成基層員工其實比誰都希望經理趕快出現的心聲

zh-tw.content:
「叫你們經理出來！」
好的，我這就去叫——
其實這句話，
我一天想喊八百遍。
您以為經理出來會站您那邊，
他其實只是負責
用比較貴的薪水，
講一模一樣的那句「不好意思」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給每個被吼著要找主管的第一線人員。祕密是：你比客人更想把經理挖出來，只是你的理由跟他不一樣。
variations: 客人喊「找你們主管」的那一刻，其實是我今天離下班最近的一次。
經理出來不會改變答案，只會用更貴的時薪講同一句「抱歉，沒辦法」。

en.content:
"Get me your manager!"
Sure, right away —
honestly, that's a sentence
I want to shout eight hundred times a day myself.
You think the manager will take your side.
He's actually just here to say
the exact same "I'm so sorry,"
only on a bigger salary.
en.editorNote: For every front-liner screamed at to "fetch a supervisor." The secret: you want the manager out there even more than the customer does — you two just have different reasons.
variations: The moment a customer yells "get your supervisor," that's the closest I've felt to clocking out all day.
The manager coming over won't change the answer — it just delivers the same "sorry, no can do" at a higher hourly rate.

items.sourceNote: internal only — 「假裝客人是對的」主管建議 vs 員工「我一天已經假裝八小時了」的對照哏，改寫成服務業自嘲心聲

zh-tw.content:
主管教我：
「遇到難搞的客人，就假裝他是對的。」
我說：
「這個我很熟，我一天已經假裝八小時了。」
這份工作最累的
從來不是站著，
是笑著。
zh-tw.editorNote: 使用場景：給每個把「配合演出」當基本功的服務業朋友。體力可以練，臉部肌肉的耐力才是真本事。
variations: 服務業的隱藏技能欄第一格，永遠是「假裝」，而且熟練度早就滿等。
客人以為他遇到很好講話的員工，其實他遇到的是一個演技很好的。

en.content:
My supervisor coached me:
"With a difficult customer, just pretend they're right."
I said:
"Oh, I've got that down — I already pretend for eight hours a day."
The hardest part of this job
was never the standing.
It's the smiling.
en.editorNote: For everyone whose core skill is "playing along." You can train your legs for the standing — it's the facial-muscle endurance that's the real qualification.
variations: First slot on a service worker's hidden skill tree is always "pretending," and it maxed out years ago.
The customer thinks he found an easygoing employee. He actually found a really good actor.
